book.name: Joshua
book.author: Unknown (traditionally attributed to Joshua, with later editorial additions)
book.dateWritten: c. 1400-1200 BC (events), with final editing perhaps in the early monarchy
book.writtenFrom: Canaan, the promised land, during and after the conquest
book.writtenTo: The people of Israel, to record the fulfillment of God's promise to give them the land of Canaan

explainer.overview: Joshua tells the story of Israel's conquest and settlement of the promised land under the leadership of Moses' successor, Joshua son of Nun. The book divides into three sections: the conquest of Canaan (chapters 1-12), the division of the land among the twelve tribes (chapters 13-21), and Joshua's farewell and covenant renewal (chapters 22-24). It records the fulfillment of God's centuries-old promise to Abraham: 'To your descendants I will give this land.'
explainer.historicalContext: After forty years of wilderness wandering, a new generation of Israelites stood on the east bank of the Jordan River. Moses had died, and Joshua was commissioned as his successor. The Canaanite city-states were weakened by Egyptian decline and internal rivalries, creating a power vacuum. The conquest was not a single campaign but a series of strategic strikes followed by a gradual process of settlement. Archaeological evidence suggests a complex period of transition in Canaan during the Late Bronze Age to Iron Age I period (c. 1200 BC).
explainer.whyItMatters: Joshua demonstrates that God keeps His promises -- even when fulfillment takes centuries. The book shows both the power of faithful obedience (Jericho, Ai after repentance) and the cost of disobedience (Ai's first defeat, the Gibeonite deception). Joshua's leadership provides a model of courageous, faith-driven action. His famous challenge -- 'Choose this day whom you will serve' -- remains one of the most direct calls to decision in all of Scripture. The book also raises difficult ethical questions about holy war and the conquest that thoughtful readers have wrestled with for millennia.
keyThemes: God's faithfulness in fulfilling His promises
Courageous obedience in the face of overwhelming odds
The importance of remembering what God has done
Holy war and the judgment of the Canaanites
The division of the land as each tribe's inheritance
The call to choose whom you will serve

people.name: Joshua
people.role: Moses' successor, military commander, leader of the conquest
people.bio: Joshua son of Nun, from the tribe of Ephraim, served as Moses' assistant from youth. He was one of only two spies (with Caleb) who gave a faithful report after scouting Canaan. He led Israel across the Jordan, conquered the major Canaanite strongholds, divided the land among the tribes, and renewed the covenant at Shechem before his death at age 110.
people.culturalContext: Joshua's original name was Hoshea ('salvation'), which Moses changed to Yehoshua ('The LORD saves'). The Greek form of his name is Jesus (Iesous), creating a typological connection between the one who led Israel into the promised land and the one who leads believers into eternal rest.

people.name: Rahab
people.role: Canaanite prostitute who hid the Israelite spies
people.bio: Rahab was a prostitute in Jericho who hid two Israelite spies and helped them escape. She professed faith in Israel's God, declaring 'The LORD your God is God in heaven above and on the earth below.' Her family was spared when Jericho fell. She married Salmon and became an ancestor of King David and, ultimately, of Jesus Christ.
people.culturalContext: Rahab's inclusion in Israel -- and later in the genealogy of Jesus (Matthew 1:5) -- demonstrates that God's grace extends beyond ethnic boundaries. A Canaanite prostitute becoming an ancestor of the Messiah is one of the Bible's most powerful statements about the scope of divine redemption.

people.name: Caleb
people.bio: Caleb son of Jephunneh, from the tribe of Judah, was one of the twelve spies sent into Canaan under Moses. Along with Joshua, he urged Israel to trust God and enter the land. For his faithfulness, God promised him a personal inheritance. At age 85, Caleb claimed Hebron, driving out the Anakim giants that had terrified the other spies forty-five years earlier.
people.culturalContext: Caleb is described as a Kenizzite, suggesting non-Israelite origins. Yet he is fully integrated into Judah and becomes one of Israel's greatest warriors. His story demonstrates that covenant identity was based on faith and loyalty, not purely on ethnicity.

people.name: Achan
people.role: Israelite who violated the ban at Jericho
people.bio: Achan son of Karmi, from the tribe of Judah, took devoted items from Jericho's spoil -- a beautiful robe, silver, and gold -- hiding them under his tent. His secret sin caused Israel's defeat at Ai and was exposed by lot-casting. Achan and his family were stoned and burned in the Valley of Achor, a name meaning 'trouble.'
people.culturalContext: Achan's story illustrates the concept of corporate responsibility in ancient Israel. One person's hidden sin affected the entire community, causing military defeat and divine anger. The severity of the punishment reflects the gravity of violating sacred war regulations.

places.name: Jericho
places.description: An ancient fortified city near the Jordan River, the first major obstacle in Israel's conquest of Canaan. Its walls famously fell after Israel marched around them for seven days.
places.culturalSignificance: Jericho's miraculous fall demonstrated that God fights for Israel. The city was placed under the ban (herem) -- completely devoted to God through destruction. It served as the 'firstfruits' of the conquest, entirely dedicated to God.

places.name: Gilgal
places.description: Israel's first camp west of the Jordan, where they were circumcised, celebrated Passover, and set up memorial stones from the Jordan crossing.
places.culturalSignificance: Gilgal served as Joshua's base of operations throughout the conquest. Its name means 'rolling away,' commemorating God's declaration: 'Today I have rolled away the reproach of Egypt from you.' It was a place of covenant renewal and remembrance.

places.name: Ai
places.description: A small city near Bethel whose name means 'ruin.' Israel was initially defeated here due to Achan's sin, then conquered it after purging the sin.
places.culturalSignificance: Ai teaches that military success depends on spiritual faithfulness, not just strategy. The contrast between the first defeat (with hidden sin) and the second victory (after repentance) is one of the book's central lessons.

places.name: Shechem
places.description: A city in the central hill country between Mount Ebal and Mount Gerizim. The site of Joshua's covenant renewal ceremony and his final address to Israel.
places.culturalSignificance: Shechem was deeply connected to Israel's history: Abraham first received God's promise there, Jacob bought land there, and Joseph's bones were buried there. Joshua's covenant renewal at Shechem tied the conquest to the patriarchal promises.

timeline.event: Israel crosses the Jordan River under Joshua's leadership
The central, southern, and northern campaigns conquer major Canaanite strongholds
The land is divided among the twelve tribes by lot at Shiloh
Joshua's farewell address and covenant renewal at Shechem

explainer.summary: Chapter 1 records God's commissioning of Joshua and the people's response. God commands Joshua three times to 'be strong and courageous,' linking courage to obedience to His law. The chapter establishes the leadership transition from Moses and the unity of all tribes for the conquest.
themes: Divine commissioning of leadership
The command to be strong and courageous
Obedience to God's word as the foundation of success
Unity among all tribes for the common mission
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 1 sets the theological foundation for the entire book: God's promises are reliable, but their fulfillment requires courageous obedience. Every subsequent event in Joshua will test whether Israel lives up to this opening commitment.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Two Hebrew words combining inner resolve (chazaq,
wordStudies.meaning: Two Hebrew words combining inner resolve (chazaq, to be firm or strong) with bold action (amatz, to be alert and courageous). Together they describe the combination of internal conviction and external action that faithful leadership requires.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To murmur, mutter, or speak quietly to oneself
wordStudies.meaning: To murmur, mutter, or speak quietly to oneself. Biblical meditation is not silent emptying of the mind but active, verbal engagement with God's word -- reading it aloud, thinking it through, and applying it.
wordStudies.significance: To murmur, mutter, or speak quietly to oneself. Biblical meditation is not silent emptying of the mi

verses.text: Now after the death of Moses the servant of Yahweh, Yahweh spoke to Joshua the son of Nun, Moses’ servant, saying,
verses.explainer: After Moses' death, God speaks directly to Joshua son of Nun, Moses' assistant. The transition of leadership is divine, not political -- God initiates, not a committee.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The transfer of leadership after Moses' death was a critical moment. In the ancient Near East, the death of a leader often triggered power struggles and fragmentation. God's direct commissioning of Joshua prevented this.

verses.text: “Moses my servant is dead. Now therefore arise, go across this Jordan, you, and all these people, to the land which I am giving to them, even to the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: God declares: 'Moses my servant is dead. Now then, you and all these people, get ready to cross the Jordan into the land I am about to give to the Israelites.' Action follows grief.

verses.text: I have given you every place that the sole of your foot will tread on, as I told Moses.
verses.explainer: God promises every place Joshua's foot treads will be his, as God promised Moses. The land is a gift, but it must be claimed through walking -- divine promise requires human action.

verses.text: From the wilderness, and this Lebanon, even to the great river, the river Euphrates, all the land of the Hittites, and to the great sea toward the going down of the sun, shall be your border.
verses.explainer: The territory is defined: from the desert and Lebanon to the Euphrates and the Mediterranean. This describes the ideal borders, fully realized only briefly under Solomon.

verses.text: No man will be able to stand before you all the days of your life. As I was with Moses, so I will be with you. I will not fail you nor forsake you.
verses.explainer: God promises: 'No one will be able to stand against you all the days of your life. As I was with Moses, so I will be with you; I will never leave you nor forsake you.' The promise of presence is the foundation.

verses.text: “Be strong and courageous; for you shall cause this people to inherit the land which I swore to their fathers to give them.
verses.explainer: 'Be strong and courageous, because you will lead these people to inherit the land I swore to their ancestors to give them.' Strength and courage are commanded, not suggested.

crossReferences.text: Moses publicly commissioned Joshua before all Israel: 'Be strong and courageous... The LORD himself goes before you.'

verses.text: Only be strong and very courageous. Be careful to observe to do according to all the law, which Moses my servant commanded you. Don’t turn from it to the right hand or to the left, that you may have good success wherever you go.
verses.explainer: 'Be strong and very courageous. Be careful to obey all the law my servant Moses gave you; do not turn from it to the right or to the left.' Courage is linked to obedience, not recklessness.

verses.text: This book of the law shall not depart from your mouth, but you shall meditate on it day and night, that you may observe to do according to all that is written in it; for then you shall make your way prosperous, and then you shall have good success.
verses.explainer: 'Keep this Book of the Law always on your lips; meditate on it day and night.' Success is defined not by military strategy but by faithfulness to God's word.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Meditating on the law 'day and night' does not mean constant reading but a life orientation. The law was to shape every decision, every action, every thought -- the framework through which all of life was interpreted.

crossReferences.text: The psalmist echoes this principle: 'Blessed is the one whose delight is in the law of the LORD, who meditates on it day and night.'

thenVsNow.then: Success in Joshua 1:8 is defined by faithfulness to God's word, not by outcomes. This redefinition of success -- internal obedience rather than external results -- challenges every culture's metrics.
thenVsNow.now: Success in Joshua 1:8 is defined by faithfulness to God's word, not by outcomes. This redefinition of success -- internal obedience rather than external results -- challenges every culture's metrics.

verses.text: Haven’t I commanded you? Be strong and courageous. Don’t be afraid. Don’t be dismayed, for Yahweh your God is with you wherever you go.”
verses.explainer: 'Have I not commanded you? Be strong and courageous. Do not be afraid; do not be discouraged, for the LORD your God will be with you wherever you go.' The third 'be strong' -- repetition underscores the command's importance.

thenVsNow.then: God's command to 'be strong and courageous' is given three times because the task ahead is genuinely frightening. Courage is not the absence of fear but obedience in the face of it.
thenVsNow.now: God's command to 'be strong and courageous' is given three times because the task ahead is genuinely frightening. Courage is not the absence of fear but obedience in the face of it.

verses.text: Then Joshua commanded the officers of the people, saying,
verses.explainer: Joshua orders the officers to go through the camp and tell the people to prepare provisions: in three days they will cross the Jordan. Command flows from divine commission to human organization.

verses.text: “Pass through the middle of the camp, and command the people, saying, ‘Prepare food; for within three days you are to pass over this Jordan, to go in to possess the land, which Yahweh your God gives you to possess it.’”
verses.explainer: The officers spread the word: 'Get your provisions ready. Three days from now you will cross the Jordan to take possession of the land the LORD your God is giving you.' The countdown begins.

verses.text: Joshua spoke to the Reubenites, and to the Gadites, and to the half-tribe of Manasseh, saying,
verses.explainer: Joshua addresses the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh, who have already received their inheritance east of the Jordan. Even those already settled must help their brothers.

crossReferences.text: Moses originally made this agreement with the eastern tribes, and Joshua now holds them to it.

verses.text: “Remember the word which Moses the servant of Yahweh commanded you, saying, ‘Yahweh your God gives you rest, and will give you this land.
verses.explainer: He reminds them of Moses' command: 'The LORD your God will give you rest and has given you this land.' Their eastern territory is already secured.

verses.text: Your wives, your little ones, and your livestock, shall live in the land which Moses gave you beyond the Jordan; but you shall pass over before your brothers armed, all the mighty men of valor, and shall help them
verses.explainer: 'Your wives, children, and livestock may stay east of the Jordan, but all your fighting men, ready for battle, must cross ahead of your brothers.' Family stays; warriors go.

verses.text: until Yahweh has given your brothers rest, as he has given you, and they have also possessed the land which Yahweh your God gives them. Then you shall return to the land of your possession, and possess it, which Moses the servant of Yahweh gave you beyond the Jordan toward the sunrise.’”
verses.explainer: The eastern tribes must help until the LORD gives rest to the other tribes. Only after the conquest is complete can the Transjordan tribes return to their inheritance.

verses.text: They answered Joshua, saying, “All that you have commanded us we will do, and wherever you send us we will go.
verses.explainer: The people answer Joshua: 'Whatever you have commanded us we will do, and wherever you send us we will go.' Total submission to Joshua's authority -- a promising start.

verses.text: Just as we listened to Moses in all things, so will we listen to you. Only may Yahweh your God be with you, as he was with Moses.
verses.explainer: 'Just as we fully obeyed Moses, so we will obey you. Only may the LORD your God be with you as he was with Moses!' Their obedience is conditional on God's presence with Joshua.

verses.text: Whoever rebels against your commandment, and doesn’t listen to your words in all that you command him shall himself be put to death. Only be strong and courageous.”
verses.explainer: 'Whoever rebels against your command and does not obey it will be put to death. Only be strong and courageous!' The people echo God's command back to Joshua.

explainer.summary: Chapter 2 tells the story of Rahab, the Canaanite prostitute who hides Israelite spies and confesses faith in Israel's God. Her remarkable theological statement -- that the LORD is God in heaven and on earth -- leads to a covenant that saves her family when Jericho falls. The scarlet cord becomes a symbol of salvation through faith.
themes: Faith from an unexpected source
God's grace crossing ethnic and moral boundaries
The scarlet cord as a sign of salvation
Courage that risks everything on faith
explainer.contextInBook: Rahab's story demonstrates that the conquest is not simply ethnic warfare but a theological event. Those who acknowledge God -- regardless of background -- can be incorporated into God's people. Her inclusion in Jesus' genealogy (Matthew 1:5) confirms this theme.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Covenant loyalty, faithful love, mercy
wordStudies.meaning: Covenant loyalty, faithful love, mercy. When Rahab asks for chesed, she is asking for the kind of steadfast commitment that characterizes God's own covenant relationships. She asks to be treated as a covenant partner, not just a useful informant.
wordStudies.significance: Covenant loyalty, faithful love, mercy. When Rahab asks for chesed, she is asking for the kind of st

wordStudies.translatedAs: A rope of crimson thread
wordStudies.meaning: A rope of crimson thread. Many interpreters see a parallel with the blood of the Passover lamb on the doorposts in Egypt -- both serve as visible signs that mark those to be spared from judgment.
wordStudies.significance: A rope of crimson thread. Many interpreters see a parallel with the blood of the Passover lamb on th

verses.text: Joshua the son of Nun secretly sent two men out of Shittim as spies, saying, “Go, view the land, including Jericho.” They went and came into the house of a prostitute whose name was Rahab, and slept there.
verses.explainer: Joshua sends two spies from Shittim to reconnoiter Jericho. They enter the house of a prostitute named Rahab and stay there. Military intelligence gathering begins the campaign.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: A prostitute's house near the city wall would have been common in ancient cities. Travelers could visit without attracting attention, making it both a logical cover for spies and a place Rahab could plausibly shelter strangers.

crossReferences.text: By faith the prostitute Rahab, because she welcomed the spies, was not killed with those who were disobedient.

verses.text: The king of Jericho was told, “Behold, men of the children of Israel came in here tonight to spy out the land.”
verses.explainer: The king of Jericho is told that Israelite spies have entered the city. He sends orders to Rahab: 'Bring out the men who came to you.' The security apparatus responds quickly.

verses.text: Jericho’s king sent to Rahab, saying, “Bring out the men who have come to you, who have entered into your house; for they have come to spy out all the land.”
verses.explainer: The king's messengers demand the spies, but Rahab has already hidden them. She lies: 'Yes, the men came to me, but I did not know where they were from.' She chooses Israel's God over her king.

verses.text: The woman took the two men and hid them. Then she said, “Yes, the men came to me, but I didn’t know where they came from.
verses.explainer: 'The men left at dusk when the city gate was about to close. I don't know which way they went. Go after them quickly.' Her deception is detailed and convincing.

verses.text: About the time of the shutting of the gate, when it was dark, the men went out. Where the men went, I don’t know. Pursue them quickly. You may catch up with them.”
verses.explainer: Rahab's story of the spies leaving at dusk sends the pursuers on a wild chase toward the Jordan fords while the spies hide safely on her roof.

verses.text: But she had brought them up to the roof, and hidden them under the stalks of flax which she had laid in order on the roof.
verses.explainer: She has taken them up to the roof and hidden them under stalks of flax laid out there to dry. Flax processing on flat rooftops was common in ancient Canaan.

verses.text: The men pursued them along the way to the fords of the Jordan River. As soon as those who pursued them had gone out, they shut the gate.
verses.explainer: The pursuers go toward the Jordan fords, and the city gate is shut behind them. The entire city is now locked down, but the spies are safely inside, not outside.

verses.text: Before they had lain down, she came up to them on the roof.
verses.explainer: Before the spies lie down for the night, Rahab comes to them on the roof. What follows is a remarkable confession of faith from a Canaanite woman.

verses.text: She said to the men, “I know that Yahweh has given you the land, and that the fear of you has fallen upon us, and that all the inhabitants of the land melt away before you.
verses.explainer: 'I know that the LORD has given you this land. A great fear of you has fallen on us. All who live in this country are melting in fear because of you.' She speaks with prophetic clarity.

verses.text: For we have heard how Yahweh dried up the water of the Red Sea before you, when you came out of Egypt; and what you did to the two kings of the Amorites, who were beyond the Jordan, to Sihon and to Og, whom you utterly destroyed.
verses.explainer: 'We have heard how the LORD dried up the water of the Red Sea and what you did to the two Amorite kings, Sihon and Og.' News of God's acts has traveled ahead of Israel.

verses.text: As soon as we had heard it, our hearts melted, and there wasn’t any more spirit in any man, because of you: for Yahweh your God, he is God in heaven above, and on earth beneath.
verses.explainer: 'When we heard of it, our hearts melted and everyone's courage failed. For the LORD your God is God in heaven above and on the earth below.' This is a full theological confession -- perhaps the most remarkable in the Old Testament coming from a Canaanite prostitute.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Rahab's confession -- 'The LORD your God is God in heaven above and on earth below' -- uses formal covenant language. This is not casual acknowledgment but deliberate theological declaration.

crossReferences.text: Rahab is listed in the genealogy of Jesus, demonstrating God's grace in incorporating outsiders into His redemptive plan.

thenVsNow.then: Rahab's story demolishes the idea that God's grace is limited to those born into the right family or nation. A Canaanite prostitute who believes is welcomed; Israelites who disobey are judged. Faith, not pedigree, determines belonging.
thenVsNow.now: Rahab's story demolishes the idea that God's grace is limited to those born into the right family or nation. A Canaanite prostitute who believes is welcomed; Israelites who disobey are judged. Faith, not pedigree, determines belonging.

verses.text: Now therefore, please swear to me by Yahweh, since I have dealt kindly with you, that you also will deal kindly with my father’s house, and give me a true sign;
verses.explainer: Rahab asks: 'Now swear to me by the LORD that you will show kindness to my family, as I have shown kindness to you. Give me a sure sign.' She negotiates from faith.

verses.text: and that you will save alive my father, my mother, my brothers, and my sisters, and all that they have, and will deliver our lives from death.”
verses.explainer: She asks that they spare her father, mother, brothers, sisters, and their families -- 'save us from death.' Her request encompasses her entire extended family.

verses.text: The men said to her, “Our life for yours, if you don’t talk about this business of ours; and it shall be, when Yahweh gives us the land, that we will deal kindly and truly with you.”
verses.explainer: The men promise: 'Our lives for your lives! If you don't tell what we are doing, we will treat you kindly when the LORD gives us the land.' A covenant of mutual protection.

verses.text: Then she let them down by a cord through the window; for her house was on the side of the wall, and she lived on the wall.
verses.explainer: Rahab lets them down by a rope through the window, for her house is built into the city wall. The wall that will fall is the very wall that enables their escape.

verses.text: She said to them, “Go to the mountain, lest the pursuers find you. Hide yourselves there three days, until the pursuers have returned. Afterward, you may go your way.”
verses.explainer: She advises them to head for the hills and hide for three days until the pursuers have returned. Her local knowledge guides their escape route.

verses.text: The men said to her, “We will be guiltless of this your oath which you’ve made us to swear.
verses.explainer: The spies set conditions: 'This oath you made us swear will not be binding on us unless you follow our instructions.' The covenant has terms.

verses.text: Behold, when we come into the land, tie this line of scarlet thread in the window which you used to let us down. Gather to yourself into the house your father, your mother, your brothers, and all your father’s household.
verses.explainer: 'When we enter the land, tie this scarlet cord in the window through which you let us down, and bring your entire family into your house.' The scarlet cord becomes a sign of salvation.

crossReferences.text: The Passover blood on the doorposts served the same function as Rahab's scarlet cord: a visible sign marking those to be spared from judgment.

verses.text: It shall be that whoever goes out of the doors of your house into the street, his blood will be on his head, and we will be guiltless. Whoever is with you in the house, his blood shall be on our head, if any hand is on him.
verses.explainer: Anyone who goes outside the house into the street forfeits protection. Anyone inside the house with the scarlet cord will be safe. The boundary of salvation is the house itself.

verses.text: But if you talk about this business of ours, then we shall be guiltless of your oath which you’ve made us to swear.”
verses.explainer: 'But if you tell what we are doing, we will be released from the oath.' Secrecy is part of the agreement.

verses.text: She said, “Let it be as you have said.” She sent them away, and they departed. Then she tied the scarlet line in the window.
verses.explainer: 'Agreed,' Rahab replies. 'Let it be as you say.' She sends them away and ties the scarlet cord in the window immediately. She acts on faith without delay.

thenVsNow.then: The scarlet cord in the window was a public act of faith. Rahab did not hide her allegiance but displayed it visibly. Genuine faith requires visible commitment, not just private belief.
thenVsNow.now: The scarlet cord in the window was a public act of faith. Rahab did not hide her allegiance but displayed it visibly. Genuine faith requires visible commitment, not just private belief.

verses.text: They went, and came to the mountain, and stayed there three days, until the pursuers had returned. The pursuers sought them all along the way, but didn’t find them.
verses.explainer: The spies go to the hills and stay three days until the pursuers return to the city. Rahab's advice proves sound.

verses.text: Then the two men returned, descended from the mountain, crossed the river, and came to Joshua the son of Nun. They told him all that had happened to them.
verses.explainer: The two men return to Joshua and report everything. They declare: 'The LORD has surely given the whole land into our hands; all the people are melting in fear because of us.'

verses.text: They said to Joshua, “Truly Yahweh has delivered all the land into our hands. Moreover, all the inhabitants of the land melt away before us.”
verses.explainer: The spies' report to Joshua confirms what Rahab said: the Canaanites' courage has failed. The psychological victory precedes the military one.

explainer.summary: Chapter 3 describes Israel's miraculous crossing of the Jordan River at flood stage. The priests carrying the ark step into the water first, and the river stops flowing, piling up upstream. The crossing echoes the Red Sea crossing and confirms Joshua's authority as Moses' successor.
themes: Faith requires stepping into the flood before it parts
The ark of God leading the way
The Jordan crossing as a second Exodus
Public confirmation of Joshua's leadership
explainer.contextInBook: The Jordan crossing is the book's first miracle and establishes the pattern: God acts when His people obey in faith. The parallel with the Red Sea crossing (Exodus 14) signals that the God of the Exodus is still active.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To set apart, make holy, or purify for sacred purp
wordStudies.meaning: To set apart, make holy, or purify for sacred purposes. The people's consecration before the crossing involved ritual washing and abstaining from activities that would create ritual impurity. It prepared them to be in God's presence.
wordStudies.significance: To set apart, make holy, or purify for sacred purposes. The people's consecration before the crossin

wordStudies.translatedAs: The gold-covered chest containing the tablets of t
wordStudies.meaning: The gold-covered chest containing the tablets of the law, representing God's presence and His covenant with Israel. It led the crossing because God Himself was leading His people into the promised land.
wordStudies.significance: The gold-covered chest containing the tablets of the law, representing God's presence and His covena

verses.text: Joshua got up early in the morning; and they moved from Shittim, and came to the Jordan, he and all the children of Israel. They camped there before they crossed over.
verses.explainer: Early in the morning, Joshua and all Israel leave Shittim and go to the Jordan, camping there before crossing. The three-day wait builds anticipation.

verses.text: After three days, the officers went through the middle of the camp;
verses.explainer: After three days, the officers go throughout the camp giving orders to the people. Detailed instructions precede the miraculous crossing.

verses.text: and they commanded the people, saying, “When you see the ark of Yahweh your God’s covenant, and the priests the Levites bearing it, then leave your place, and follow it.
verses.explainer: 'When you see the ark of the covenant of the LORD being carried by the Levitical priests, you are to move out and follow it.' The ark leads; the people follow at a distance.

verses.text: Yet there shall be a space between you and it of about two thousand cubits by measure—don’t come closer to it—that you may know the way by which you must go; for you have not passed this way before.”
verses.explainer: They must keep a distance of about two thousand cubits between themselves and the ark. 'Do not go near it.' Then you will know which way to go, since you have never been this way before.

verses.text: Joshua said to the people, “Sanctify yourselves; for tomorrow Yahweh will do wonders among you.”
verses.explainer: Joshua tells the people: 'Consecrate yourselves, for tomorrow the LORD will do amazing things among you.' Spiritual preparation precedes miraculous intervention.

thenVsNow.then: Consecration before miracle -- preparation before power. God does not pour His power into unprepared vessels. The spiritual discipline of preparation is not optional but essential.
thenVsNow.now: Consecration before miracle -- preparation before power. God does not pour His power into unprepared vessels. The spiritual discipline of preparation is not optional but essential.

verses.text: Joshua spoke to the priests, saying, “Take up the ark of the covenant, and cross over before the people.” They took up the ark of the covenant, and went before the people.
verses.explainer: Joshua tells the priests: 'Take up the ark of the covenant and walk ahead of the people.' The priests carry the ark to the river's edge.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Joshua, “Today I will begin to magnify you in the sight of all Israel, that they may know that as I was with Moses, so I will be with you.
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Joshua: 'Today I will begin to exalt you in the eyes of all Israel, so they may know that I am with you as I was with Moses.' Joshua's authority is publicly confirmed.

verses.text: You shall command the priests who bear the ark of the covenant, saying, ‘When you come to the brink of the waters of the Jordan, you shall stand still in the Jordan.’”
verses.explainer: 'Tell the priests who carry the ark to stand in the Jordan when they reach the edge of its waters.' They must step into the water before it parts.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The priests' willingness to step into floodwaters while carrying the most sacred object in Israel required extraordinary courage and faith. The waters did not part before they stepped in -- they parted because they stepped in.

verses.text: Joshua said to the children of Israel, “Come here, and hear the words of Yahweh your God.”
verses.explainer: Joshua tells the Israelites: 'Come here and listen to the words of the LORD your God.' The people are gathered to hear God's promise before witnessing His act.

verses.text: Joshua said, “By this you shall know that the living God is among you, and that he will without fail drive the Canaanite, and the Hittite, and the Hivite, and the Perizzite, and the Girgashite, and the Amorite, and the Jebusite out from before you.
verses.explainer: Joshua declares: 'This is how you will know that the living God is among you: he will certainly drive out the Canaanites, Hittites, Hivites, Perizzites, Girgashites, Amorites, and Jebusites.' Seven nations will fall.

verses.text: Behold, the ark of the covenant of the Lord of all the earth passes over before you into the Jordan.
verses.explainer: 'The ark of the covenant of the Lord of all the earth will go into the Jordan ahead of you.' God -- the Lord of all the earth, not just Israel -- leads the way.

verses.text: Now therefore take twelve men out of the tribes of Israel, for every tribe a man.
verses.explainer: Joshua selects twelve men, one from each tribe, who will play a role in the memorial that follows the crossing.

verses.text: It shall be that when the soles of the feet of the priests who bear the ark of Yahweh, the Lord of all the earth, rest in the waters of the Jordan, that the waters of the Jordan will be cut off. The waters that come down from above shall stand in one heap.”
verses.explainer: 'When the priests step into the Jordan, its waters flowing downstream will be cut off and stand up in a heap.' The promise is specific and verifiable.

verses.text: When the people moved from their tents to pass over the Jordan, the priests who bore the ark of the covenant being before the people,
verses.explainer: The people break camp to cross the Jordan, with the priests carrying the ark ahead of them. The march begins.

verses.text: and when those who bore the ark had come to the Jordan, and the feet of the priests who bore the ark had dipped in the edge of the water (for the Jordan overflows all its banks all the time of harvest),
verses.explainer: The Jordan is at flood stage during harvest season. When the priests' feet touch the water's edge, the waters stop flowing. Faith requires stepping into the flood.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The Jordan crossing at flood stage was militarily impossible without divine intervention. Ancient armies avoided river crossings during flood season. God chose the hardest possible conditions to demonstrate His power.

thenVsNow.then: The priests had to step into the flood before it stopped. God often requires an act of faith before He acts. We want the miracle first, then the obedience; God usually reverses that order.
thenVsNow.now: The priests had to step into the flood before it stopped. God often requires an act of faith before He acts. We want the miracle first, then the obedience; God usually reverses that order.

verses.text: the waters which came down from above stood, and rose up in one heap, a great way off, at Adam, the city that is beside Zarethan; and those that went down toward the sea of the Arabah, even the Salt Sea, were wholly cut off. Then the people passed over near Jericho.
verses.explainer: The water from upstream stops flowing and piles up at a town called Adam. The water flowing to the Dead Sea is completely cut off. The people cross opposite Jericho on dry ground.

crossReferences.text: God parted the Red Sea for Moses; now He parts the Jordan for Joshua. The parallel establishes continuity between God's acts of deliverance.

verses.text: The priests who bore the ark of Yahweh’s covenant stood firm on dry ground in the middle of the Jordan; and all Israel crossed over on dry ground, until all the nation had passed completely over the Jordan.
verses.explainer: The priests carrying the ark stand firm on dry ground in the middle of the Jordan while all Israel crosses on dry ground. The priests remain stationary as a marker of divine presence until every person has crossed.

explainer.summary: Chapter 4 establishes the memorial stones at Gilgal, taken from the Jordan riverbed. The twelve stones serve as a perpetual teaching tool: when future generations ask 'What do these stones mean?', parents will tell the story of God's power. The chapter emphasizes that remembering God's acts is essential to sustaining faith.
themes: The importance of memorials and remembrance
Teaching the next generation
Physical objects anchoring spiritual memory
The universal and internal purposes of God's acts
explainer.contextInBook: The memorial stones connect to the book's larger theme of remembering God's faithfulness. Joshua will reference God's past acts throughout the book, building the case that the God who acted in the past can be trusted for the future.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A tangible reminder designed to provoke memory and
wordStudies.meaning: A tangible reminder designed to provoke memory and teaching. Biblical memorials are not passive monuments but active teaching tools that generate conversation between generations about God's faithfulness.
wordStudies.significance: A tangible reminder designed to provoke memory and teaching. Biblical memorials are not passive monu

wordStudies.translatedAs: Not terror but reverent awe combined with trust
wordStudies.meaning: Not terror but reverent awe combined with trust. To 'fear the LORD' is to recognize His power and respond with worship, obedience, and reliance on His character.
wordStudies.significance: Not terror but reverent awe combined with trust. To 'fear the LORD' is to recognize His power and re

verses.text: When all the nation had completely crossed over the Jordan, Yahweh spoke to Joshua, saying,
verses.explainer: When the whole nation has finished crossing the Jordan, the LORD speaks to Joshua about creating a memorial. The crossing is complete; now it must be remembered.

verses.text: “Take twelve men out of the people, a man out of every tribe,
verses.explainer: Joshua is told to choose twelve men, one from each tribe, for a specific task. Each tribe must participate in the memorial.

verses.text: and command them, saying, ‘Take from out of the middle of the Jordan, out of the place where the priests’ feet stood firm, twelve stones, and carry them over with you, and lay them down in the place where you’ll camp tonight.’”
verses.explainer: 'Tell them to take twelve stones from the middle of the Jordan, from right where the priests stood, and carry them to where you camp tonight.' Stones from the river bed become monuments.

verses.text: Then Joshua called the twelve men, whom he had prepared of the children of Israel, a man out of every tribe.
verses.explainer: Joshua summons the twelve men he had appointed and gives them their instructions. The plan connects physical objects to spiritual memory.

verses.text: Joshua said to them, “Cross before the ark of Yahweh your God into the middle of the Jordan, and each of you pick up a stone and put it on your shoulder, according to the number of the tribes of the children of Israel;
verses.explainer: 'Each of you take a stone on your shoulder, one for each of the tribes of Israel.' The stones are personally carried -- each tribe bears its own memorial.

verses.text: that this may be a sign among you, that when your children ask in the future, saying, ‘What do you mean by these stones?’
verses.explainer: 'These stones will serve as a sign among you. In the future, when your children ask, What do these stones mean?' The memorial is designed to provoke questions from the next generation.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Memorial stones were common in the ancient Near East. Stone circles, standing stones, and cairns served as boundary markers, treaty witnesses, and religious markers. Israel's stones uniquely pointed to God's specific historical acts.

crossReferences.text: Moses commanded: 'When your son asks you... tell him: We were slaves in Egypt and the LORD brought us out.' The same question-and-answer pattern for passing faith to the next generation.

thenVsNow.then: The memorial stones teach us that faith must be intentionally transmitted. It does not pass automatically from generation to generation but requires concrete teaching moments, physical reminders, and honest conversations about what God has done.
thenVsNow.now: The memorial stones teach us that faith must be intentionally transmitted. It does not pass automatically from generation to generation but requires concrete teaching moments, physical reminders, and honest conversations about what God has done.

verses.text: then you shall tell them, ‘Because the waters of the Jordan were cut off before the ark of Yahweh’s covenant. When it crossed over the Jordan, the waters of the Jordan were cut off. These stones shall be for a memorial to the children of Israel forever.’”
verses.explainer: 'Tell them that the flow of the Jordan was cut off before the ark of the LORD. These stones are to be a memorial to the people of Israel forever.' Physical objects anchor spiritual memory.

verses.text: The children of Israel did as Joshua commanded, and took up twelve stones out of the middle of the Jordan, as Yahweh spoke to Joshua, according to the number of the tribes of the children of Israel. They carried them over with them to the place where they camped, and laid them down there.
verses.explainer: The Israelites do as Joshua commands. They take twelve stones from the middle of the Jordan and carry them to their camp at Gilgal.

verses.text: Joshua set up twelve stones in the middle of the Jordan, in the place where the feet of the priests who bore the ark of the covenant stood; and they are there to this day.
verses.explainer: Joshua also sets up twelve stones in the middle of the Jordan, at the spot where the priests stood. They are still there 'to this day.' A double memorial -- one visible, one submerged.

verses.text: For the priests who bore the ark stood in the middle of the Jordan, until everything was finished that Yahweh commanded Joshua to speak to the people, according to all that Moses commanded Joshua; and the people hurried and passed over.
verses.explainer: The priests carrying the ark remain standing in the middle of the Jordan until everything the LORD commanded Joshua is done. The priests' faithful stationary position holds the waters back.

verses.text: When all the people had completely crossed over, Yahweh’s ark crossed over, with the priests, in the presence of the people.
verses.explainer: When everyone has crossed, the ark and priests cross over in the presence of the people. The ark is both first and last -- leading and concluding.

verses.text: The children of Reuben, and the children of Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh, crossed over armed before the children of Israel, as Moses spoke to them.
verses.explainer: The men of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh cross armed, in front of the Israelites, as Moses had directed. The eastern tribes honor their commitment.

verses.text: About forty thousand men, ready and armed for war passed over before Yahweh to battle, to the plains of Jericho.
verses.explainer: About forty thousand armed men cross before the LORD to the plains of Jericho, ready for battle. The military force assembled is substantial.

verses.text: On that day, Yahweh magnified Joshua in the sight of all Israel; and they feared him, as they feared Moses, all the days of his life.
verses.explainer: That day the LORD exalts Joshua in the sight of all Israel. They revere him all the days of his life, just as they revered Moses. Joshua's authority is publicly established.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Joshua, saying,
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Joshua to command the priests to come up out of the Jordan. The final instruction before the waters return.

verses.text: “Command the priests who bear the ark of the testimony, that they come up out of the Jordan.”
verses.explainer: Joshua commands the priests to come up. The moment of obedience triggers the natural response -- the waters will return.

verses.text: Joshua therefore commanded the priests, saying, “Come up out of the Jordan!”
verses.explainer: The priests come up out of the Jordan carrying the ark. The moment their feet reach dry ground, the Jordan's waters return to flood stage. The miracle ends precisely when the need ends.

verses.text: When the priests who bore the ark of Yahweh’s covenant had come up out of the middle of the Jordan, and the soles of the priests’ feet had been lifted up to the dry ground, the waters of the Jordan returned to their place, and went over all its banks, as before.
verses.explainer: On the tenth day of the first month, the people come up from the Jordan and camp at Gilgal on the eastern border of Jericho. The date connects to Passover preparation.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The tenth day of the first month was the day when the Passover lamb was selected (Exodus 12:3). Arriving on this date connected the Jordan crossing to the Exodus -- the new deliverance echoes the original.

crossReferences.text: On the tenth day of the first month, each family selected a Passover lamb. Israel's arrival at Gilgal on this date is not coincidental.

verses.text: The people came up out of the Jordan on the tenth day of the first month, and encamped in Gilgal, on the east border of Jericho.
verses.explainer: Joshua sets up the twelve stones at Gilgal. The memorial stands at the first camp in the promised land.

verses.text: Joshua set up those twelve stones, which they took out of the Jordan, in Gilgal.
verses.explainer: Joshua tells the people: 'In the future when your descendants ask their parents what these stones mean, tell them Israel crossed the Jordan on dry ground.' The catechetical purpose is central.

verses.text: He spoke to the children of Israel, saying, “When your children ask their fathers in time to come, saying, ‘What do these stones mean?’
verses.explainer: 'The LORD your God dried up the Jordan before you until you crossed over, just as he dried up the Red Sea.' The explicit connection between Jordan and Red Sea is made.

verses.text: Then you shall let your children know, saying, ‘Israel came over this Jordan on dry land.
verses.explainer: 'He did this so that all the peoples of the earth might know that the hand of the LORD is powerful.' The miracle has a universal audience -- not just Israel but all nations.

thenVsNow.then: The dual purpose of the crossing -- external witness to all peoples and internal reverence within Israel -- shows that God's acts always have both dimensions. What God does for His people is also a message to the watching world.
thenVsNow.now: The dual purpose of the crossing -- external witness to all peoples and internal reverence within Israel -- shows that God's acts always have both dimensions. What God does for His people is also a message to the watching world.

verses.text: For Yahweh your God dried up the waters of the Jordan from before you, until you had crossed over, as Yahweh your God did to the Red Sea, which he dried up from before us, until we had crossed over;
verses.explainer: 'He did this so that you might always fear the LORD your God.' The internal purpose: reverence for God based on experienced deliverance.

verses.text: that all the peoples of the earth may know that Yahweh’s hand is mighty; that you may fear Yahweh your God forever.’”
verses.explainer: The memorial's dual purpose is declared: that all peoples know God's power, and that Israel always reverence Him. External testimony and internal worship are both served.

explainer.summary: Chapter 5 records four events that prepare Israel for the conquest: mass circumcision restoring the covenant sign, the celebration of Passover in the promised land, the cessation of manna, and Joshua's encounter with the commander of the LORD's army. Each event marks a transition from wilderness wandering to the promised land.
themes: Covenant renewal before conquest
The end of wilderness provisions and the beginning of land-based life
God is not on our side -- we must be on His
Holy ground demands holy people
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 5 is a pivot point: wilderness identity gives way to land identity. Circumcision, Passover, and the end of manna all mark endings and beginnings. The commander's appearance reframes the conquest as God's war, not Israel's.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The shame or stigma of having been slaves
wordStudies.meaning: The shame or stigma of having been slaves. Circumcision 'rolled away' this reproach by reestablishing Israel's identity as God's covenant people -- no longer slaves but free inheritors of the promise.
wordStudies.significance: The shame or stigma of having been slaves. Circumcision 'rolled away' this reproach by reestablishin

wordStudies.greek: Commander of the army of the LORD (sar tseva YHWH)
wordStudies.transliteration: Commander of the army of the LORD (sar tseva YHWH)
wordStudies.translatedAs: A divine military figure, often understood as a th
wordStudies.meaning: A divine military figure, often understood as a theophany or an angelic commander of heavenly forces. His presence indicates that the conquest will be fought on two levels: visible and invisible, human and divine.
wordStudies.significance: A divine military figure, often understood as a theophany or an angelic commander of heavenly forces

verses.text: When all the kings of the Amorites, who were beyond the Jordan westward, and all the kings of the Canaanites, who were by the sea, heard how Yahweh had dried up the waters of the Jordan from before the children of Israel, until we had crossed over, their heart melted, and there was no more spirit in them, because of the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: When all the Amorite and Canaanite kings hear how the LORD dried up the Jordan, their hearts melt and they lose courage. The psychological impact precedes the military campaign.

verses.text: At that time, Yahweh said to Joshua, “Make flint knives, and circumcise again the sons of Israel the second time.”
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Joshua to circumcise the Israelite males with flint knives. The wilderness generation had not been circumcised -- the covenant sign had been neglected for forty years.

verses.text: Joshua made himself flint knives, and circumcised the sons of Israel at the hill of the foreskins.
verses.explainer: Joshua circumcises them at Gibeath Haaraloth ('Hill of Foreskins'). The entire male population undergoes the covenant ritual before the conquest begins.

verses.text: This is the reason Joshua circumcised them: all the people who came out of Egypt, who were males, even all the men of war, died in the wilderness along the way, after they came out of Egypt.
verses.explainer: The reason for circumcision: all the men of military age who left Egypt had died in the wilderness because of disobedience. The old generation perished; the new generation must be marked.

verses.text: For all the people who came out were circumcised; but all the people who were born in the wilderness along the way as they came out of Egypt had not been circumcised.
verses.explainer: All the people who came out of Egypt had been circumcised, but all born during the wilderness wandering had not. Forty years of neglect must be corrected.

verses.text: For the children of Israel walked forty years in the wilderness, until all the nation, even the men of war who came out of Egypt, were consumed, because they didn’t listen to Yahweh’s voice. Yahweh swore to them that he wouldn’t let them see the land which Yahweh swore to their fathers that he would give us, a land flowing with milk and honey.
verses.explainer: The Israelites had moved about in the wilderness forty years until the disobedient generation perished. God had sworn they would not see the land. The purging of the unfaithful generation is complete.

verses.text: Their children, whom he raised up in their place, were circumcised by Joshua; for they were uncircumcised, because they had not circumcised them on the way.
verses.explainer: Their children, raised up in their place, are now circumcised by Joshua. They had not been circumcised on the way. The covenant is renewed for a new generation entering a new land.

verses.text: When they were done circumcising the whole nation, they stayed in their places in the camp until they were healed.
verses.explainer: After circumcision, the entire nation remains in camp until they are healed. They are temporarily vulnerable -- an act of faith, trusting God for protection during recovery.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Circumcising the entire male population left Israel's army temporarily incapacitated, as the Shechemites discovered when Simeon and Levi attacked them on the third day after circumcision (Genesis 34:25). This act of obedience required trusting God for military protection.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Joshua, “Today I have rolled away the reproach of Egypt from you.” Therefore the name of that place was called Gilgal, to this day.
verses.explainer: The LORD says: 'Today I have rolled away the reproach of Egypt from you.' The place is called Gilgal ('rolling'). The shame of slavery is officially ended.

verses.text: The children of Israel encamped in Gilgal. They kept the Passover on the fourteenth day of the month at evening in the plains of Jericho.
verses.explainer: The Israelites celebrate Passover on the evening of the fourteenth day of the month at Gilgal, on the plains of Jericho. The first Passover in the promised land.

crossReferences.text: The original Passover in Egypt celebrated deliverance from slavery. This Passover in Canaan celebrates the arrival in the promised land -- the bookend of the Exodus story.

verses.text: They ate unleavened cakes and parched grain of the produce of the land on the next day after the Passover, in the same day.
verses.explainer: The day after Passover, they eat some of the produce of the land: unleavened bread and roasted grain. They eat the food of Canaan for the first time.

verses.text: The manna ceased on the next day, after they had eaten of the produce of the land. The children of Israel didn’t have manna any more; but they ate of the fruit of the land of Canaan that year.
verses.explainer: The manna stops the day after they eat the produce of the land. No more manna appears; from that year on, the Israelites eat the food of Canaan. The miracle of provision gives way to the gift of the land.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The cessation of manna marks one of the sharpest transitions in Israel's history. For forty years, God fed them miraculously; now they will eat by farming. Divine provision changes form but does not end.

thenVsNow.then: The cessation of manna teaches that God's provision changes form as circumstances change. What was needed in the wilderness is replaced by what is needed in the land. God's faithfulness adapts to our season.
thenVsNow.now: The cessation of manna teaches that God's provision changes form as circumstances change. What was needed in the wilderness is replaced by what is needed in the land. God's faithfulness adapts to our season.

verses.text: When Joshua was by Jericho, he lifted up his eyes and looked, and behold, a man stood in front of him with his sword drawn in his hand. Joshua went to him and said to him, “Are you for us, or for our enemies?”
verses.explainer: Near Jericho, Joshua looks up and sees a man standing before him with a drawn sword. Joshua approaches and asks: 'Are you for us or for our enemies?' The question assumes the stranger must take sides.

verses.text:
He said, “No; but I have come now as commander of Yahweh’s army.”
Joshua fell on his face to the earth, and worshiped, and asked him, “What does my lord say to his servant?”
verses.explainer: 'Neither,' the figure replies. 'I have come as commander of the army of the LORD.' The answer shatters Joshua's binary framework -- God is not on Israel's side; Israel must be on God's side.

thenVsNow.then: The commander's answer -- 'Neither' -- is one of the most important theological corrections in Scripture. God does not join our side; we join His. The question is never 'Is God with us?' but 'Are we with God?'
thenVsNow.now: The commander's answer -- 'Neither' -- is one of the most important theological corrections in Scripture. God does not join our side; we join His. The question is never 'Is God with us?' but 'Are we with God?'

verses.text: The prince of Yahweh’s army said to Joshua, “Take off your shoes; for the place on which you stand is holy.” Joshua did so.
verses.explainer: The commander says: 'Take off your sandals, for the place where you are standing is holy.' Joshua falls facedown in worship. The command echoes Moses at the burning bush.

crossReferences.text: God told Moses at the burning bush: 'Take off your sandals, for the place where you are standing is holy ground.' The identical command links Joshua to Moses.

explainer.summary: Chapter 6 tells the famous story of Jericho's fall. Israel marches silently around the city for six days, then seven times on the seventh day. When the trumpets sound and the people shout, the walls collapse. Rahab and her family are saved; everything else is devoted to God. Jericho becomes the firstfruits of the conquest.
themes: Victory through obedience, not military strategy
The power of faith expressed in action
The ban (herem) as complete devotion to God
God's faithfulness to Rahab's covenant
explainer.contextInBook: Jericho is the paradigmatic victory: obedience to God's unusual instructions produces miraculous results. Every subsequent battle will be measured against this standard. When Israel obeys completely, God acts powerfully.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Complete dedication to God through destruction
wordStudies.meaning: Complete dedication to God through destruction. Objects under herem could not be used, taken, or kept. Jericho as the first city conquered was treated like firstfruits -- entirely given to God. The herem ensured Israel could not profit from the conquest materially.
wordStudies.significance: Complete dedication to God through destruction. Objects under herem could not be used, taken, or kep

wordStudies.translatedAs: A loud, joyful battle cry
wordStudies.meaning: A loud, joyful battle cry. The shout combined a military war cry with a shout of worship. It was both a declaration of faith in God's victory and a terrifying sound for the enemy.
wordStudies.significance: A loud, joyful battle cry. The shout combined a military war cry with a shout of worship. It was bot

verses.text: Now Jericho was tightly shut up because of the children of Israel. No one went out, and no one came in.
verses.explainer: Jericho is tightly shut up because of the Israelites. No one goes in or out. The city is sealed in fear, awaiting attack by an army led by an invisible God.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Joshua, “Behold, I have given Jericho into your hand, with its king and the mighty men of valor.
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Joshua: 'I have delivered Jericho into your hands, along with its king and its fighting men.' Note the past tense: 'I have delivered' -- the victory is already accomplished in God's mind.

verses.text: All of your men of war shall march around the city, going around the city once. You shall do this six days.
verses.explainer: 'March around the city once with all the armed men. Do this for six days.' The battle plan is a religious procession, not a military assault.

verses.text: Seven priests shall bear seven trumpets of rams’ horns before the ark. On the seventh day, you shall march around the city seven times, and the priests shall blow the trumpets.
verses.explainer: 'Seven priests shall carry trumpets of rams' horns before the ark. On the seventh day, march around seven times, with the priests blowing the trumpets.' The number seven -- completion and perfection -- dominates.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Seven days, seven priests, seven trumpets, seven circuits -- the number seven pervades the account. In Hebrew thought, seven represents completion and divine perfection. The entire event is structured around divine design.

verses.text: It shall be that when they make a long blast with the ram’s horn, and when you hear the sound of the trumpet, all the people shall shout with a great shout; and the city wall shall fall down flat, and the people shall go up, every man straight in front of him.”
verses.explainer: 'When you hear a long blast on the trumpets, have the whole army shout. The wall of the city will collapse and the army will go up, everyone straight in.' Shout first, then attack -- faith precedes victory.

verses.text: Joshua the son of Nun called the priests, and said to them, “Take up the ark of the covenant, and let seven priests bear seven trumpets of rams’ horns before Yahweh’s ark.”
verses.explainer: Joshua calls the priests and gives the LORD's instructions. The divine plan is transmitted through human chain of command.

verses.text: They said to the people, “Advance! March around the city, and let the armed men pass on before Yahweh’s ark.”
verses.explainer: Joshua commands the people: 'Advance! March around the city, with an armed guard going ahead of the ark of the LORD.' The military escorts the sacred, not the other way around.

verses.text: It was so, that when Joshua had spoken to the people, the seven priests bearing the seven trumpets of rams’ horns before Yahweh advanced and blew the trumpets, and the ark of Yahweh’s covenant followed them.
verses.explainer: When Joshua finishes speaking, the seven priests with trumpets advance before the LORD, blowing as they go. The ark of the LORD follows them.

verses.text: The armed men went before the priests who blew the trumpets, and the ark went after them. The trumpets sounded as they went.
verses.explainer: The armed guard marches ahead of the priests; the rear guard follows the ark. The trumpets keep sounding as they march. A military procession organized around the ark.

verses.text: Joshua commanded the people, saying, “You shall not shout, nor let your voice be heard, neither shall any word proceed out of your mouth, until the day I tell you to shout. Then you shall shout.”
verses.explainer: Joshua commands the army: 'Do not give a war cry, do not raise your voices, do not say a word until the day I tell you to shout. Then shout!' Silence builds tension and discipline.

verses.text: So he caused Yahweh’s ark to go around the city, going about it once. Then they came into the camp, and stayed in the camp.
verses.explainer: The ark of the LORD is carried around the city once. Then the army returns to camp for the night. Day one: one circuit in silence.

verses.text: Joshua rose early in the morning, and the priests took up Yahweh’s ark.
verses.explainer: Joshua gets up early the next morning. The priests take up the ark. The daily procession continues with disciplined repetition.

verses.text: The seven priests bearing the seven trumpets of rams’ horns in front of Yahweh’s ark went on continually, and blew the trumpets. The armed men went in front of them. The rear guard came after Yahweh’s ark. The trumpets sounded as they went.
verses.explainer: The seven priests carry the seven trumpets before the ark, marching and blowing. Armed men go ahead and behind. Day two follows the same pattern.

verses.text: The second day they marched around the city once, and returned into the camp. They did this six days.
verses.explainer: On the second day they march around the city once and return to camp. They do this for six days. Six days of patient, silent obedience.

thenVsNow.then: The six days of silent marching required extraordinary discipline. Doing the same thing repeatedly without visible results tests faith more than a single dramatic act. Most of faithfulness is quiet repetition.
thenVsNow.now: The six days of silent marching required extraordinary discipline. Doing the same thing repeatedly without visible results tests faith more than a single dramatic act. Most of faithfulness is quiet repetition.

verses.text: On the seventh day, they rose early at the dawning of the day, and marched around the city in the same way seven times. Only on this day they marched around the city seven times.
verses.explainer: On the seventh day, they rise at dawn and march around the city seven times. Only on this day do they march seven times. The climax is approaching.

verses.text: At the seventh time, when the priests blew the trumpets, Joshua said to the people, “Shout, for Yahweh has given you the city!
verses.explainer: On the seventh circuit, when the priests sound the long blast, Joshua commands: 'Shout! For the LORD has given you the city!' The command to shout is also a declaration of faith.

verses.text: The city shall be devoted, even it and all that is in it, to Yahweh. Only Rahab the prostitute shall live, she and all who are with her in the house, because she hid the messengers that we sent.
verses.explainer: The city is to be devoted to the LORD -- under the ban (herem). Only Rahab and those in her house shall live. Everything else is devoted to destruction. Jericho is the firstfruits of conquest.

verses.text: But as for you, only keep yourselves from what is devoted to destruction, lest when you have devoted it, you take of the devoted thing; so you would make the camp of Israel accursed, and trouble it.
verses.explainer: 'But keep away from the devoted things, so that you will not bring about your own destruction.' The warning about taking devoted items foreshadows Achan's sin in chapter 7.

verses.text: But all the silver, gold, and vessels of brass and iron, are holy to Yahweh. They shall come into Yahweh’s treasury.”
verses.explainer: 'All the silver and gold and the articles of bronze and iron are sacred to the LORD. They must go into the treasury of the LORD.' Metals are preserved for God's use.

verses.text: So the people shouted and the priests blew the trumpets. When the people heard the sound of the trumpet, the people shouted with a great shout, and the wall fell down flat, so that the people went up into the city, every man straight in front of him, and they took the city.
verses.explainer: When the trumpets sound, the army shouts. The wall collapses. Everyone charges straight in, and they take the city. The most famous wall-fall in history.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Archaeological evidence at Tell es-Sultan (Jericho) shows collapsed walls, though the dating remains debated. The city's small size (about 6 acres) meant that the march around it took only about 30-40 minutes per circuit.

crossReferences.text: By faith the walls of Jericho fell, after the army had marched around them for seven days.

thenVsNow.then: Jericho's fall teaches that God's methods often defy human logic. Marching and shouting are not standard siege tactics. Yet obedience to God's unusual instructions accomplished what conventional warfare could not.
thenVsNow.now: Jericho's fall teaches that God's methods often defy human logic. Marching and shouting are not standard siege tactics. Yet obedience to God's unusual instructions accomplished what conventional warfare could not.

verses.text: They utterly destroyed all that was in the city, both man and woman, both young and old, and ox, sheep, and donkey, with the edge of the sword.
verses.explainer: They devote the city to the LORD, destroying with the sword every living thing: men, women, young, old, cattle, sheep, donkeys. The herem is carried out completely.

verses.text: Joshua said to the two men who had spied out the land, “Go into the prostitute’s house, and bring the woman and all that she has out from there, as you swore to her.”
verses.explainer: Joshua tells the two spies: 'Go into the prostitute's house and bring her out, as you swore to her.' The covenant with Rahab is honored.

verses.text: The young men who were spies went in, and brought out Rahab with her father, her mother, her brothers, and all that she had. They also brought out all of her relatives, and they set them outside of the camp of Israel.
verses.explainer: The young men who had spied bring out Rahab, her father, mother, brothers, and all who belong to her. They place her family outside the camp of Israel, initially separated but soon integrated.

verses.text: They burned the city with fire, and all that was in it. Only they put the silver, the gold, and the vessels of brass and of iron into the treasury of Yahweh’s house.
verses.explainer: They burn the city and everything in it, except the metals which go into the LORD's treasury. Jericho is completely destroyed as devoted to God.

verses.text: But Rahab the prostitute, her father’s household, and all that she had, Joshua saved alive. She lives in the middle of Israel to this day, because she hid the messengers, whom Joshua sent to spy out Jericho.
verses.explainer: Joshua spares Rahab and her family because she hid the spies. She lives among the Israelites to this day. The Canaanite prostitute becomes an Israelite by faith.

verses.text: Joshua commanded them with an oath at that time, saying, “Cursed is the man before Yahweh, who rises up and builds this city Jericho. With the loss of his firstborn he will lay its foundation, and with the loss of his youngest son he will set up its gates.”
verses.explainer: Joshua pronounces a curse: 'Cursed before the LORD is anyone who rebuilds Jericho. At the cost of his firstborn he will lay its foundations; at the cost of his youngest he will set up its gates.' The city is permanently banned.

crossReferences.text: Centuries later, Hiel of Bethel rebuilt Jericho at the cost of his firstborn and youngest son, fulfilling Joshua's curse precisely.

verses.text: So Yahweh was with Joshua; and his fame was in all the land.
verses.explainer: The LORD is with Joshua, and his fame spreads throughout the land. The successful conquest of Jericho establishes Joshua's reputation as a God-empowered leader.

explainer.summary: Chapter 7 tells how Achan's secret theft of devoted items from Jericho causes Israel's defeat at Ai. God reveals the sin through lot-casting, and Achan confesses. The purging of sin through Achan's execution restores Israel's relationship with God. The chapter demonstrates that hidden sin has public consequences.
themes: Hidden sin with public consequences
Corporate responsibility for individual actions
The pattern of seeing, coveting, and taking
Purging sin to restore covenant relationship
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 7 is the dark counterpart to chapter 6. Jericho fell through obedience; Ai succeeds through Israel's disobedience. The contrast teaches that spiritual faithfulness, not military superiority, determines the outcome.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Items placed under the ban and belonging exclusive
wordStudies.meaning: Items placed under the ban and belonging exclusively to God. Taking herem items was not merely theft but sacrilege -- stealing from God Himself. Achan's sin violated the most sacred boundary of the conquest.
wordStudies.significance: Items placed under the ban and belonging exclusively to God. Taking herem items was not merely theft

wordStudies.translatedAs: Means 'Valley of Trouble
wordStudies.meaning: Means 'Valley of Trouble.' Named after Achan's sin, it later becomes a symbol of hope: Hosea 2:15 promises that the Valley of Achor will become 'a door of hope.' Even places of judgment can be transformed.
wordStudies.significance: Means 'Valley of Trouble.' Named after Achan's sin, it later becomes a symbol of hope: Hosea 2:15 pr

verses.text: But the children of Israel committed a trespass in the devoted things; for Achan, the son of Carmi, the son of Zabdi, the son of Zerah, of the tribe of Judah, took some of the devoted things. Therefore Yahweh’s anger burned against the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: But the Israelites are unfaithful regarding the devoted things. Achan takes some of what was devoted. The LORD's anger burns against Israel. One man's sin affects the entire nation.

thenVsNow.then: One person's hidden sin caused thirty-six deaths and a national defeat. The interconnectedness of community means that private choices have public consequences. We are never as isolated as we imagine.
thenVsNow.now: One person's hidden sin caused thirty-six deaths and a national defeat. The interconnectedness of community means that private choices have public consequences. We are never as isolated as we imagine.

verses.text:
Joshua sent men from Jericho to Ai, which is beside Beth Aven, on the east side of Bethel, and spoke to them, saying, “Go up and spy out the land.”
The men went up and spied out Ai.
verses.explainer: Joshua sends men from Jericho to Ai and tells them to spy out the region. The reconnaissance reports a small, apparently easy target.

verses.text: They returned to Joshua, and said to him, “Don’t let all the people go up; but let about two or three thousand men go up and strike Ai. Don’t make all the people to toil there, for there are only a few of them.”
verses.explainer: The spies return: 'Not all the army will have to go up. Send two or three thousand men. Don't weary the whole army, for only a few people live there.' Overconfidence after Jericho leads to underestimation.

verses.text: So about three thousand men of the people went up there, and they fled before the men of Ai.
verses.explainer: About three thousand men go up, but the men of Ai rout them. Israel's army flees from Ai's defenders. The shock is total.

verses.text: The men of Ai struck about thirty-six men of them, and they chased them from before the gate even to Shebarim, and struck them at the descent. The hearts of the people melted, and became like water.
verses.explainer: The men of Ai kill about thirty-six Israelites and chase the rest from the city gate to Shebarim. The people's hearts melt and become like water. The hunters become the hunted.

verses.text: Joshua tore his clothes, and fell to the earth on his face before Yahweh’s ark until the evening, he and the elders of Israel; and they put dust on their heads.
verses.explainer: Joshua tears his clothes, falls facedown before the ark until evening, and sprinkles dust on his head. The elders do the same. The traditional gestures of grief and mourning.

verses.text: Joshua said, “Alas, Lord Yahweh, why have you brought this people over the Jordan at all, to deliver us into the hand of the Amorites, to cause us to perish? I wish that we had been content and lived beyond the Jordan!
verses.explainer: Joshua cries out: 'Ah, Sovereign LORD, why did you ever bring this people across the Jordan to deliver us into the hands of the Amorites to destroy us?' His complaint echoes the wilderness complaints he once fought against.

verses.text: Oh, Lord, what shall I say, after Israel has turned their backs before their enemies!
verses.explainer: 'What can I say now that Israel has been routed by its enemies?' Joshua is devastated by both the defeat and what it implies about God's presence.

verses.text: For the Canaanites and all the inhabitants of the land will hear of it, and will surround us, and cut off our name from the earth. What will you do for your great name?”
verses.explainer: 'The Canaanites will hear about this and surround us and wipe out our name from the earth. What then will you do for your own great name?' Joshua argues that God's reputation is at stake.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Joshua, “Get up! Why have you fallen on your face like that?
verses.explainer: The LORD responds bluntly: 'Stand up! What are you doing down on your face?' God's answer is not comforting but confrontational. Prayer without action is insufficient.

verses.text: Israel has sinned. Yes, they have even transgressed my covenant which I commanded them. Yes, they have even taken some of the devoted things, and have also stolen, and also deceived. They have even put it among their own stuff.
verses.explainer: 'Israel has sinned. They have violated my covenant. They have taken some of the devoted things. They have stolen, lied, and put them with their own possessions.' The sin is cataloged in escalating terms.

verses.text: Therefore the children of Israel can’t stand before their enemies. They turn their backs before their enemies, because they have become devoted for destruction. I will not be with you any more, unless you destroy the devoted things from among you.
verses.explainer: 'Israel cannot stand before their enemies because they themselves have become devoted to destruction.' The sin has made Israel itself herem -- subject to the same judgment as the Canaanites.

verses.text: “Get up! Sanctify the people, and say, ‘Sanctify yourselves for tomorrow, for Yahweh, the God of Israel, says, “There is a devoted thing among you, Israel. You cannot stand before your enemies until you take away the devoted thing from among you.”
verses.explainer: 'Go, consecrate the people. Tell them to consecrate themselves for tomorrow.' Purification must precede the next battle.
