verses.text: “‘In the morning therefore you shall be brought near by your tribes. It shall be that the tribe which Yahweh selects shall come near by families. The family which Yahweh selects shall come near by households. The household which Yahweh selects shall come near man by man.
verses.explainer: 'In the morning present yourselves tribe by tribe. The tribe the LORD points out will come forward clan by clan.' The investigation narrows through divine lot-casting.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The lot-casting process (tribe, clan, family, individual) was understood as God directing the outcome. This was not random chance but divine identification. The gradual narrowing built dramatic tension and gave each group the opportunity to confess.

verses.text: It shall be, that he who is taken with the devoted thing shall be burned with fire, he and all that he has, because he has transgressed Yahweh’s covenant, and because he has done a disgraceful thing in Israel.’”
verses.explainer: Whoever is caught with devoted things will be burned with fire, along with everything belonging to him. The punishment is severe because the covenant itself has been violated.

verses.text: So Joshua rose up early in the morning and brought Israel near by their tribes. The tribe of Judah was selected.
verses.explainer: Early the next morning, Joshua has Israel come forward tribe by tribe. The tribe of Judah is selected. The lot begins to narrow.

verses.text: He brought near the family of Judah; and he selected the family of the Zerahites. He brought near the family of the Zerahites man by man, and Zabdi was selected.
verses.explainer: The clans of Judah come forward, and the Zerahites are selected. Then the Zerahite families, and Zimri's family is selected. The net tightens.

verses.text: He brought near his household man by man, and Achan, the son of Carmi, the son of Zabdi, the son of Zerah, of the tribe of Judah, was selected.
verses.explainer: Joshua has Zimri's family come forward man by man, and Achan son of Karmi, son of Zimri, son of Zerah, of the tribe of Judah, is selected. The hidden sin is publicly exposed.

verses.text: Joshua said to Achan, “My son, please give glory to Yahweh, the God of Israel, and make confession to him. Tell me now what you have done! Don’t hide it from me!”
verses.explainer: Joshua says to Achan: 'My son, give glory to the LORD. Tell me what you have done; do not hide it from me.' The invitation to confess is genuine -- Joshua calls him 'my son.'

verses.text: Achan answered Joshua, and said, “I have truly sinned against Yahweh, the God of Israel, and this is what I have done.
verses.explainer: Achan confesses: 'It is true! I have sinned against the LORD, the God of Israel.' His confession is direct and specific.

verses.text: When I saw among the plunder a beautiful Babylonian robe, two hundred shekels of silver, and a wedge of gold weighing fifty shekels, then I coveted them and took them. Behold, they are hidden in the ground in the middle of my tent, with the silver under it.”
verses.explainer: 'When I saw among the plunder a beautiful robe from Babylonia, two hundred shekels of silver, and a bar of gold, I coveted them and took them. They are hidden in the ground inside my tent.' Seeing, coveting, taking -- the same pattern as Eden.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Achan's sin follows the exact pattern of the original sin in Genesis 3: she saw the fruit was desirable, she took it. Achan saw, coveted, and took. The pattern of temptation is consistent throughout Scripture.

crossReferences.text: Eve 'saw that the fruit of the tree was good... she took some and ate it.' The identical pattern: seeing, desiring, taking.

thenVsNow.then: Achan's confession reveals the universal sequence of temptation: 'I saw... I coveted... I took.' Temptation begins with the eyes, moves to the heart, and ends with the hands. Breaking the chain at any point prevents the sin.
thenVsNow.now: Achan's confession reveals the universal sequence of temptation: 'I saw... I coveted... I took.' Temptation begins with the eyes, moves to the heart, and ends with the hands. Breaking the chain at any point prevents the sin.

verses.text: So Joshua sent messengers, and they ran to the tent. Behold, it was hidden in his tent, with the silver under it.
verses.explainer: Joshua sends messengers who run to the tent and find everything hidden in the ground, with the silver underneath. The evidence confirms the confession.

verses.text: They took them from the middle of the tent, and brought them to Joshua and to all the children of Israel. They laid them down before Yahweh.
verses.explainer: They take the items from the tent and bring them to Joshua and all Israel. They spread them out before the LORD. The stolen items are exposed publicly before God.

verses.text: Joshua, and all Israel with him, took Achan the son of Zerah, the silver, the robe, the wedge of gold, his sons, his daughters, his cattle, his donkeys, his sheep, his tent, and all that he had; and they brought them up to the valley of Achor.
verses.explainer: Joshua and all Israel take Achan with the stolen goods, his sons and daughters, livestock, tent, and everything he has, to the Valley of Achor. The entire household is brought.

verses.text: Joshua said, “Why have you troubled us? Yahweh will trouble you today.” All Israel stoned him with stones, and they burned them with fire and stoned them with stones.
verses.explainer: Joshua asks: 'Why have you brought this trouble on us? The LORD will bring trouble on you today.' Then all Israel stones him and burns everything. The purging is complete.

verses.text: They raised over him a great heap of stones that remains to this day. Yahweh turned from the fierceness of his anger. Therefore the name of that place was called “The valley of Achor” to this day.
verses.explainer: They raise a large pile of rocks over Achan. The LORD turns from his fierce anger. The Valley of Achor ('trouble') is named after the event. The memorial is a warning, not a celebration.

crossReferences.text: God promises to make the Valley of Achor 'a door of hope.' The place of judgment becomes a place of future restoration.

explainer.summary: Chapter 8 describes the successful conquest of Ai through an ambush strategy and concludes with a covenant renewal ceremony at Mount Ebal. After the previous defeat caused by sin, the restored nation achieves victory through a combination of divine empowerment and tactical wisdom. The covenant ceremony on Ebal connects military conquest to spiritual commitment.
themes: Restoration after failure
Divine power working through human strategy
The covenant ceremony connecting conquest to commitment
Reading the law to the entire community
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 8 resolves the crisis of chapter 7 and demonstrates the pattern: sin causes defeat, repentance restores the relationship, and renewed obedience brings victory. The Ebal ceremony fulfills Moses' command from Deuteronomy 27 and ties the conquest to the covenant.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A short spear or curved sword
wordStudies.meaning: A short spear or curved sword. Joshua's raised javelin served as both a military signal and a symbolic parallel to Moses raising his staff during the battle with Amalek (Exodus 17:11). The leader's gesture channels divine power.
wordStudies.significance: A short spear or curved sword. Joshua's raised javelin served as both a military signal and a symbol

wordStudies.translatedAs: Stones in their natural state, not shaped by human
wordStudies.meaning: Stones in their natural state, not shaped by human tools. The prohibition on cutting stones for altars (Exodus 20:25) ensured that human artistry did not become the focus. The altar's simplicity pointed worshippers to God, not to the craftsmanship.
wordStudies.significance: Stones in their natural state, not shaped by human tools. The prohibition on cutting stones for alta

verses.text: Yahweh said to Joshua, “Don’t be afraid, and don’t be dismayed. Take all the warriors with you, and arise, go up to Ai. Behold, I have given into your hand the king of Ai, with his people, his city, and his land.
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Joshua: 'Do not be afraid; do not be discouraged. Take the whole army with this time and go up and attack Ai.' After purging the sin, God renews the commission.

verses.text: You shall do to Ai and her king as you did to Jericho and her king, except you shall take its goods and its livestock for yourselves. Set an ambush for the city behind it.”
verses.explainer: God gives permission to take the plunder and livestock this time. Unlike Jericho, Ai's spoils are available. The army is to set an ambush behind the city.

verses.text: So Joshua arose, with all the warriors, to go up to Ai. Joshua chose thirty thousand men, the mighty men of valor, and sent them out by night.
verses.explainer: Joshua selects thirty thousand of his best fighting men and sends them out at night. The night deployment sets the ambush in position before dawn.

verses.text: He commanded them, saying, “Behold, you shall lie in ambush against the city, behind the city. Don’t go very far from the city, but all of you be ready.
verses.explainer: Joshua instructs: 'Lie in ambush behind the city, not far from it. All of you be on the alert.' Tactical precision replaces the miraculous approach used at Jericho.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The ambush at Ai is conventional military strategy -- unlike Jericho's miraculous approach. God uses both supernatural miracles and human tactical wisdom. The conquest does not always look the same.

thenVsNow.then: Ai is conquered through military strategy rather than miraculous intervention. God works through both the supernatural and the natural. Expecting every challenge to be met miraculously is as wrong as expecting none to be.
thenVsNow.now: Ai is conquered through military strategy rather than miraculous intervention. God works through both the supernatural and the natural. Expecting every challenge to be met miraculously is as wrong as expecting none to be.

verses.text: I and all the people who are with me will approach to the city. It shall happen, when they come out against us, as at the first, that we will flee before them.
verses.explainer: 'I and all those with me will advance on the city. When the men come out against us as before, we will flee from them.' Joshua plans a deliberate feigned retreat.

verses.text: They will come out after us, until we have drawn them away from the city; for they will say, ‘They flee before us, like the first time.’ So we will flee before them,
verses.explainer: 'They will pursue us until we have lured them away from the city, thinking we are running as before.' The strategy exploits the enemy's overconfidence from their first victory.

verses.text: and you shall rise up from the ambush, and take possession of the city; for Yahweh your God will deliver it into your hand.
verses.explainer: 'Then you rise from your ambush and take the city. The LORD your God will give it into your hand.' Human strategy and divine empowerment work together.

verses.text: It shall be, when you have seized the city, that you shall set the city on fire. You shall do this according to Yahweh’s word. Behold, I have commanded you.”
verses.explainer: 'When you have taken the city, set it on fire.' Clear, specific instructions prevent the confusion that could result from battlefield improvisation.

verses.text: Joshua sent them out; and they went to set up the ambush, and stayed between Bethel and Ai, on the west side of Ai; but Joshua stayed among the people that night.
verses.explainer: Joshua sends them to the place of ambush between Bethel and Ai, west of Ai. He spends the night with the people in the valley.

verses.text: Joshua rose up early in the morning, mustered the people, and went up, he and the elders of Israel, before the people to Ai.
verses.explainer: Early the next morning, Joshua musters his men. He and the elders of Israel march ahead of the army to Ai. The commander leads from the front.

verses.text: All the people, even the men of war who were with him, went up, and came near, and came before the city, and encamped on the north side of Ai. Now there was a valley between him and Ai.
verses.explainer: The army advances and camps north of Ai, with a valley between them and the city. The main force is positioned to be seen.

verses.text: He took about five thousand men, and set them in ambush between Bethel and Ai, on the west side of the city.
verses.explainer: Joshua stations about five thousand men in ambush between Bethel and Ai, west of the city. A secondary ambush force supplements the main ambush.

verses.text: So they set the people, even all the army who was on the north of the city, and their ambush on the west of the city; and Joshua went that night into the middle of the valley.
verses.explainer: The army is positioned: main force north, ambush west. Joshua goes into the valley that night. All forces are in place for the dawn attack.

verses.text: When the king of Ai saw it, they hurried and rose up early, and the men of the city went out against Israel to battle, he and all his people, at the time appointed, before the Arabah; but he didn’t know that there was an ambush against him behind the city.
verses.explainer: When the king of Ai sees the Israelites, he and his army hurry out early in the morning to meet them in battle at a set place overlooking the Arabah. He does not know about the ambush.

verses.text: Joshua and all Israel made as if they were beaten before them, and fled by the way of the wilderness.
verses.explainer: Joshua and all Israel let themselves be driven back before them and flee toward the wilderness. The feigned retreat begins.

verses.text: All the people who were in the city were called together to pursue after them. They pursued Joshua, and were drawn away from the city.
verses.explainer: All the men of Ai are called to pursue them. They pursue Joshua and are lured away from the city. Every soldier leaves Ai to join the chase.

verses.text: There was not a man left in Ai or Beth El who didn’t go out after Israel. They left the city open, and pursued Israel.
verses.explainer: Not a man remains in Ai or Bethel who has not gone out after Israel. They leave the city wide open. The trap is perfectly set.

verses.text:
Yahweh said to Joshua, “Stretch out the javelin that is in your hand toward Ai, for I will give it into your hand.”
Joshua stretched out the javelin that was in his hand toward the city.
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Joshua: 'Hold out toward Ai the javelin that is in your hand, for into your hand I will deliver the city.' Joshua holds out his javelin. The raised weapon becomes a signal and a symbol of divine authority.

crossReferences.text: When Moses held up his hands, Israel prevailed; when he lowered them, Amalek prevailed. Joshua's raised javelin follows the same pattern.

verses.text: The ambush arose quickly out of their place, and they ran as soon as he had stretched out his hand, and entered into the city, and took it. They hurried and set the city on fire.
verses.explainer: As soon as Joshua stretches out his javelin, the men in ambush rise quickly and rush to seize the city. They enter it and set it on fire immediately.

verses.text: When the men of Ai looked behind them, they saw, and behold, the smoke of the city ascended up to heaven, and they had no power to flee this way or that way. The people who fled to the wilderness turned back on the pursuers.
verses.explainer: The men of Ai look back and see smoke rising from the city. They have no strength to flee because the Israelites in retreat turn and attack.

verses.text: When Joshua and all Israel saw that the ambush had taken the city, and that the smoke of the city ascended, then they turned again, and killed the men of Ai.
verses.explainer: When Joshua and all Israel see the smoke signal, they turn and attack Ai's army. The ambush force comes out of the city against them. Ai's army is caught between two forces.

verses.text: The others came out of the city against them, so they were in the middle of Israel, some on this side, and some on that side. They struck them, so that they let none of them remain or escape.
verses.explainer: Israel strikes them down from both sides. No one survives or escapes. The king of Ai is captured alive.

verses.text: They captured the king of Ai alive, and brought him to Joshua.
verses.explainer: The king of Ai is brought to Joshua as a captive. The rest of Ai's forces are destroyed in open field.

verses.text: When Israel had finished killing all the inhabitants of Ai in the field, in the wilderness in which they pursued them, and they had all fallen by the edge of the sword, until they were consumed, all Israel returned to Ai, and struck it with the edge of the sword.
verses.explainer: After killing all the inhabitants of Ai in the fields and wilderness, every Israelite returns to Ai and puts it to the sword. The city itself is taken.

verses.text: All that fell that day, both of men and women, were twelve thousand, even all the men of Ai.
verses.explainer: Twelve thousand men and women fall that day -- all the people of Ai. The destruction is total.

verses.text: For Joshua didn’t draw back his hand, with which he stretched out the javelin, until he had utterly destroyed all the inhabitants of Ai.
verses.explainer: Joshua does not draw back the hand holding the javelin until he has devoted all the inhabitants of Ai to destruction. Like Moses holding up his staff during the Amalekite battle.

verses.text: Israel took for themselves only the livestock and the goods of that city, according to Yahweh’s word which he commanded Joshua.
verses.explainer: Israel takes the livestock and plunder of the city for themselves, as the LORD had commanded Joshua. This time, legitimate plunder is permitted.

verses.text: So Joshua burned Ai, and made it a heap forever, even a desolation, to this day.
verses.explainer: Joshua burns Ai and makes it a permanent heap of ruins, desolate to this day. The city lives up to its name: Ai means 'ruin.'

verses.text: He hanged the king of Ai on a tree until the evening, and at sundown Joshua commanded, and they took his body down from the tree, and threw it at the entrance of the gate of the city, and raised a great heap of stones on it that remains to this day.
verses.explainer: He impales the body of the king of Ai on a pole and leaves it until evening. At sunset, Joshua orders the body taken down and thrown at the city gate entrance, covered with stones. The law requires bodies to be removed before nightfall.

crossReferences.text: A body hung on a pole must be removed before sunset so as not to defile the land. Joshua observes this law even for enemy kings.

verses.text: Then Joshua built an altar to Yahweh, the God of Israel, on Mount Ebal,
verses.explainer: Then Joshua builds an altar to the LORD on Mount Ebal. The scene shifts dramatically from military victory to covenant renewal. The altar is built in obedience to Moses' command.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The covenant renewal at Ebal fulfilled Moses' command in Deuteronomy 27:2-8. The journey from Ai to Ebal (about 20 miles through potentially hostile territory) shows Joshua's commitment to obeying every detail of Moses' instruction.

crossReferences.text: Moses commanded that on the day Israel crossed the Jordan, they should set up plastered stones on Mount Ebal and build an altar. Joshua fulfills this command.

verses.text: as Moses the servant of Yahweh commanded the children of Israel, as it is written in the book of the law of Moses, an altar of uncut stones, on which no one had lifted up any iron. They offered burnt offerings on it to Yahweh and sacrificed peace offerings.
verses.explainer: The altar is built of uncut stones, on which no iron tool has been used, as Moses commanded. They offer burnt offerings and fellowship offerings. The sacrifice follows ancient instructions.

verses.text: He wrote there on the stones a copy of Moses’ law, which he wrote in the presence of the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: On the stones Joshua copies the law of Moses in the presence of the Israelites. The law is publicly inscribed -- visible and accessible to all.

verses.text: All Israel, their elders and officers, and their judges, stood on both sides of the ark before the priests the Levites, who carried the ark of Yahweh’s covenant, the foreigner as well as the native; half of them in front of Mount Gerizim, and half of them in front of Mount Ebal, as Moses the servant of Yahweh had commanded at the first, that they should bless the people of Israel.
verses.explainer: All Israel, with their elders, officials, and judges, stands on both sides of the ark, facing the Levitical priests. The assembly divides between Mount Ebal and Mount Gerizim, half on each side.

verses.text: Afterward he read all the words of the law, the blessing and the curse, according to all that is written in the book of the law.
verses.explainer: Joshua reads all the words of the law -- the blessings and the curses -- just as it is written in the Book of the Law. Every word is read publicly.

verses.text: There was not a word of all that Moses commanded, which Joshua didn’t read before all the assembly of Israel, with the women, the little ones, and the foreigners who were among them.
verses.explainer: Not a word of all that Moses commanded is left unread. Joshua reads it to the whole assembly, including women, children, and foreigners living among them. The law is for everyone.

thenVsNow.then: Reading the entire law to the whole community -- including women, children, and foreigners -- ensures that no one is excluded from knowing God's expectations. Ignorance of the law is prevented by public reading.
thenVsNow.now: Reading the entire law to the whole community -- including women, children, and foreigners -- ensures that no one is excluded from knowing God's expectations. Ignorance of the law is prevented by public reading.

explainer.summary: Chapter 9 tells how the Gibeonites deceive Israel into making a peace treaty by pretending to be from a distant country. Israel examines the evidence but fails to consult God. When the deception is discovered, the oath is honored but the Gibeonites are made permanent servants at the sanctuary.
themes: The danger of deciding without consulting God
The binding nature of oaths, even when deceived
Human cleverness versus divine wisdom
Survival through submission
explainer.contextInBook: The Gibeonite deception is the conquest's first major mistake. The failure to 'inquire of the LORD' (v.14) introduces a compromised situation that will have consequences for centuries, including Saul's later persecution of the Gibeonites (2 Samuel 21).

wordStudies.translatedAs: To seek divine guidance through prayer, the Urim a
wordStudies.meaning: To seek divine guidance through prayer, the Urim and Thummim, or prophetic consultation. Israel's failure to inquire is the pivotal error -- they trusted their own judgment when they should have asked God.
wordStudies.significance: To seek divine guidance through prayer, the Urim and Thummim, or prophetic consultation. Israel's fa

wordStudies.translatedAs: A binding agreement ratified by oath
wordStudies.meaning: A binding agreement ratified by oath. Once sworn in God's name, the treaty could not be broken without incurring divine wrath, regardless of the circumstances under which it was made.
wordStudies.significance: A binding agreement ratified by oath. Once sworn in God's name, the treaty could not be broken witho

verses.text: When all the kings who were beyond the Jordan, in the hill country, and in the lowland, and on all the shore of the great sea in front of Lebanon, the Hittite, the Amorite, the Canaanite, the Perizzite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite, heard of it
verses.explainer: When all the kings west of the Jordan hear about Israel's victories, they form a unified coalition. The victories at Jericho and Ai have forced a response.

verses.text: they gathered themselves together to fight with Joshua and with Israel, with one accord.
verses.explainer: The kings of the Hittites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites unite to fight Israel. The fragmented city-states attempt collective defense.

verses.text: But when the inhabitants of Gibeon heard what Joshua had done to Jericho and to Ai,
verses.explainer: But the people of Gibeon hear what Joshua did to Jericho and Ai and resort to a different strategy. Instead of fighting, they choose deception.

verses.text: they also resorted to a ruse, and went and made as if they had been ambassadors, and took old sacks on their donkeys, and old, torn-up and bound up wine skins,
verses.explainer: They pretend to be travelers from a distant country: loading their donkeys with worn-out sacks and old wineskins, cracked and mended.

verses.text: and old and patched shoes on their feet, and wore old garments. All the bread of their food supply was dry and moldy.
verses.explainer: They wear old, patched sandals and worn-out clothes. All the bread of their food supply is dry and moldy. Every detail is carefully staged to create the illusion of a long journey.

verses.text: They went to Joshua at the camp at Gilgal, and said to him and to the men of Israel, “We have come from a far country. Now therefore make a covenant with us.”
verses.explainer: They go to Joshua at the camp at Gilgal and say: 'We have come from a distant country; make a treaty with us.' The request exploits Israel's obligation to treat distant peoples differently from Canaanite neighbors.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Gibeonite deception exploited a provision in Deuteronomy 20:10-15 that allowed peace treaties with distant cities but not with nearby Canaanite cities. Their ruse was designed to fall under the 'distant city' category.

crossReferences.text: Moses distinguished between distant cities (which could be offered peace) and nearby Canaanite cities (which must be destroyed). The Gibeonites exploited this distinction.

verses.text: The men of Israel said to the Hivites, “What if you live among us? How could we make a covenant with you?”
verses.explainer: The Israelites say to the Hivites: 'But perhaps you live near us. How then can we make a treaty with you?' Suspicion exists but is not pursued sufficiently.

verses.text:
They said to Joshua, “We are your servants.”
Joshua said to them, “Who are you? Where do you come from?”
verses.explainer: The Gibeonites say: 'We are your servants.' Joshua asks: 'Who are you and where do you come from?' He asks the right questions but accepts false answers.

verses.text: They said to him, “Your servants have come from a very far country because of the name of Yahweh your God; for we have heard of his fame, all that he did in Egypt,
verses.explainer: They claim: 'Your servants have come from a very distant country because of the fame of the LORD your God. We heard reports of all he did in Egypt.' They use theological language to gain trust.

verses.text: and all that he did to the two kings of the Amorites who were beyond the Jordan, to Sihon king of Heshbon and to Og king of Bashan, who was at Ashtaroth.
verses.explainer: They cite God's victories over Sihon and Og -- deliberately omitting Jericho and Ai (too recent for 'distant' travelers to know about). Their deception is carefully calibrated.

verses.text: Our elders and all the inhabitants of our country spoke to us, saying, ‘Take supplies in your hand for the journey, and go to meet them. Tell them, “We are your servants. Now make a covenant with us.”’
verses.explainer: 'Our elders and all those living in our country said: Take provisions and go meet them and say, We are your servants; make a treaty with us.' They claim official authorization.

verses.text: This our bread we took hot for our supplies out of our houses on the day we went out to go to you; but now, behold, it is dry, and has become moldy.
verses.explainer: 'This bread was warm when we packed it. Now see how dry and moldy it is.' The prop evidence seems convincing.

verses.text: These wine skins, which we filled, were new; and behold, they are torn. These our garments and our shoes have become old because of the very long journey.”
verses.explainer: 'These wineskins were new but are now cracked. Our clothes and sandals are worn out by the long journey.' The visual evidence supports the lie.

verses.text: The men sampled their provisions, and didn’t ask counsel from Yahweh’s mouth.
verses.explainer: The Israelites sample the Gibeonites' provisions but do not inquire of the LORD. This is the critical failure: human judgment without divine guidance.

thenVsNow.then: The most consequential line in the chapter: 'They did not inquire of the LORD.' Every major mistake in Joshua can be traced to acting without seeking God's guidance. Human judgment, no matter how reasonable, is insufficient for decisions with eternal consequences.
thenVsNow.now: The most consequential line in the chapter: 'They did not inquire of the LORD.' Every major mistake in Joshua can be traced to acting without seeking God's guidance. Human judgment, no matter how reasonable, is insufficient for decisions with eternal consequences.

verses.text: Joshua made peace with them, and made a covenant with them, to let them live. The princes of the congregation swore to them.
verses.explainer: Joshua makes a peace treaty with them and agrees to let them live. The leaders of the assembly ratify it with an oath. The covenant is sealed.

verses.text: At the end of three days after they had made a covenant with them, they heard that they were their neighbors, and that they lived among them.
verses.explainer: Three days later, Israel learns that the Gibeonites are actually neighbors living nearby. The deception is discovered too late.

verses.text: The children of Israel traveled and came to their cities on the third day. Now their cities were Gibeon, Chephirah, Beeroth, and Kiriath Jearim.
verses.explainer: Israel sets out and reaches the Gibeonite cities on the third day: Gibeon, Kephirah, Beeroth, and Kiriath-jearim. The truth is visible within a day's march.

verses.text: The children of Israel didn’t strike them, because the princes of the congregation had sworn to them by Yahweh, the God of Israel. All the congregation murmured against the princes.
verses.explainer: Israel does not attack them because the leaders have sworn an oath to them by the LORD. The community grumbles against the leaders for the oath.

verses.text: But all the princes said to all the congregation, “We have sworn to them by Yahweh, the God of Israel. Now therefore we may not touch them.
verses.explainer: The leaders explain: 'We have sworn an oath by the LORD, the God of Israel. We cannot touch them now.' Oaths made before God are binding, even when obtained by deception.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The binding nature of oaths in the ancient Near East cannot be overstated. Breaking an oath sworn in a deity's name was believed to bring divine punishment. Israel honored the oath even though it was obtained fraudulently.

crossReferences.text: Centuries later, Saul broke the treaty with the Gibeonites, bringing famine on Israel. God held Israel accountable for the oath even generations later.

verses.text: We will do this to them, and let them live; lest wrath be on us, because of the oath which we swore to them.”
verses.explainer: 'This is what we will do: we will let them live, so that wrath will not fall on us for breaking the oath.' Pragmatism and reverence for oaths combine.

verses.text: The princes said to them, “Let them live, so they became wood cutters and drawers of water for all the congregation, as the princes had spoken to them.”
verses.explainer: 'Let them live, but let them be woodcutters and water carriers for the entire assembly.' The Gibeonites are spared but given menial service roles.

verses.text: Joshua called for them, and he spoke to them, saying, “Why have you deceived us, saying, ‘We are very far from you,’ when you live among us?
verses.explainer: Joshua summons the Gibeonites: 'Why did you deceive us by saying you live far away when you actually live near us?' The confrontation is direct.

verses.text: Now therefore you are cursed, and some of you will never fail to be slaves, both wood cutters and drawers of water for the house of my God.”
verses.explainer: 'You are now under a curse: you will always serve as woodcutters and water carriers for the house of my God.' Their service is redirected to the tabernacle.

verses.text: They answered Joshua, and said, “Because your servants were certainly told how Yahweh your God commanded his servant Moses to give you all the land, and to destroy all the inhabitants of the land from before you. Therefore we were very afraid for our lives because of you, and have done this thing.
verses.explainer: The Gibeonites explain: 'We were clearly told that the LORD your God had commanded Moses to give you all the land and destroy all its inhabitants. We feared for our lives.' Their motive was survival.

verses.text: Now, behold, we are in your hand. Do to us as it seems good and right to you to do.”
verses.explainer: 'We are now in your hands. Do to us whatever seems good and right to you.' They submit completely to Israel's judgment.

verses.text: He did so to them, and delivered them out of the hand of the children of Israel, so that they didn’t kill them.
verses.explainer: Joshua saves them from the Israelites, and they are not killed. He makes them woodcutters and water carriers for the community and for the altar of the LORD at the place God chooses.

verses.text: That day Joshua made them wood cutters and drawers of water for the congregation and for Yahweh’s altar to this day, in the place which he should choose.
verses.explainer: That is what they are to this day. The Gibeonites serve permanently at the sanctuary -- a role that, while servile, keeps them close to God's house.

thenVsNow.then: The Gibeonites' deception, while morally questionable, preserved their lives. Sometimes survival requires creativity. Israel's response -- honoring the oath despite the deception -- shows integrity, while the Gibeonites' assignment to sanctuary service hints at redemptive purpose.
thenVsNow.now: The Gibeonites' deception, while morally questionable, preserved their lives. Sometimes survival requires creativity. Israel's response -- honoring the oath despite the deception -- shows integrity, while the Gibeonites' assignment to sanctuary service hints at redemptive purpose.

explainer.summary: Chapter 10 describes the southern campaign, triggered by the Amorite coalition's attack on Gibeon. Joshua's forced night march, divine hailstones, and the sun standing still produce a decisive victory over five kings. Joshua then systematically conquers the major southern cities, subduing the entire region.
themes: God fighting for Israel with supernatural weapons
The long day -- God listening to a human command
Systematic military conquest after initial breakthrough
Divine warrior theology
explainer.contextInBook: The southern campaign completes the second phase of the conquest. With the central highlands (Jericho, Ai) and the south secured, only the northern coalition remains. The sun standing still is the book's most dramatic miracle after Jericho.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To be silent, cease, or stop
wordStudies.meaning: To be silent, cease, or stop. Joshua commands the sun to 'be silent' or 'cease moving.' Whether this describes an actual astronomical event, a refraction phenomenon, or poetic language for divine intervention remains debated, but the text presents it as a unique historical event.
wordStudies.significance: To be silent, cease, or stop. Joshua commands the sun to 'be silent' or 'cease moving.' Whether this

wordStudies.translatedAs: Large stones from the sky, described as divine wea
wordStudies.meaning: Large stones from the sky, described as divine weapons. God uses the forces of nature as His army. The hailstones kill more enemies than Israel's swords, demonstrating that God is the primary warrior.
wordStudies.significance: Large stones from the sky, described as divine weapons. God uses the forces of nature as His army. T

verses.text: Now when Adoni-Zedek king of Jerusalem heard how Joshua had taken Ai, and had utterly destroyed it; as he had done to Jericho and her king, so he had done to Ai and her king; and how the inhabitants of Gibeon had made peace with Israel, and were among them,
verses.explainer: Adoni-zedek king of Jerusalem hears that Joshua has taken Ai and devoted it to destruction, and that Gibeon has made peace with Israel. Gibeon's defection alarms the southern coalition.

verses.text: they were very afraid, because Gibeon was a great city, as one of the royal cities, and because it was greater than Ai, and all its men were mighty.
verses.explainer: Jerusalem is alarmed because Gibeon is an important city, like a royal city, larger than Ai, and all its men are good fighters. A major military power has switched sides.

verses.text: Therefore Adoni-Zedek king of Jerusalem sent to Hoham king of Hebron, Piram king of Jarmuth, Japhia king of Lachish, and Debir king of Eglon, saying,
verses.explainer: Adoni-zedek appeals to four other Amorite kings -- of Hebron, Jarmuth, Lachish, and Eglon -- to join him in attacking Gibeon. The coalition targets the traitor, not Israel directly.

verses.text: “Come up to me and help me. Let us strike Gibeon; for they have made peace with Joshua and with the children of Israel.”
verses.explainer: The five Amorite kings combine forces and attack Gibeon. Punishing Gibeon's defection is their immediate goal; containing Israel is the strategic objective.

verses.text: Therefore the five kings of the Amorites, the king of Jerusalem, the king of Hebron, the king of Jarmuth, the king of Lachish, and the king of Eglon, gathered themselves together and went up, they and all their armies, and encamped against Gibeon, and made war against it.
verses.explainer: The Gibeonites send an urgent message to Joshua at Gilgal: 'Do not abandon your servants. Come up quickly and save us! All the Amorite kings from the hill country have joined forces against us.'

verses.text: The men of Gibeon sent to Joshua at the camp at Gilgal, saying, “Don’t abandon your servants! Come up to us quickly and save us! Help us; for all the kings of the Amorites that dwell in the hill country have gathered together against us.”
verses.explainer: Joshua marches all night from Gilgal to Gibeon -- about 25 miles uphill. The LORD tells him: 'Do not be afraid of them; I have given them into your hand. Not one of them will be able to withstand you.'

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: A forced night march of 25 miles uphill from Gilgal (near sea level) to Gibeon (2,500 feet elevation) was an extraordinary feat of endurance. The army arrived exhausted but achieved complete surprise.

thenVsNow.then: The forced night march from Gilgal to Gibeon reminds us that faith often requires exhausting effort. Joshua did not sit at camp waiting for a miracle; he marched all night. Divine intervention and human exertion work together.
thenVsNow.now: The forced night march from Gilgal to Gibeon reminds us that faith often requires exhausting effort. Joshua did not sit at camp waiting for a miracle; he marched all night. Divine intervention and human exertion work together.

verses.text: So Joshua went up from Gilgal, he, and the whole army with him, including all the mighty men of valor.
verses.explainer: Joshua takes the entire army, including the best fighting men, on the forced march from Gilgal. The surprise attack requires speed and stamina.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Joshua, “Don’t fear them, for I have delivered them into your hands. Not a man of them will stand before you.”
verses.explainer: The LORD promises Joshua: 'Do not be afraid of them; I have given them into your hand.' Divine assurance precedes the battle.

verses.text: Joshua therefore came to them suddenly. He marched from Gilgal all night.
verses.explainer: After marching all night, Joshua takes the five kings by surprise. The forced night march achieves complete tactical surprise.

verses.text: Yahweh confused them before Israel. He killed them with a great slaughter at Gibeon, and chased them by the way of the ascent of Beth Horon, and struck them to Azekah and to Makkedah.
verses.explainer: The LORD throws the Amorites into confusion before Israel. Joshua inflicts heavy casualties at Gibeon, pursuing them along the road going up to Beth-horon. God fights with nature and panic.

crossReferences.text: Isaiah calls God's acts at Gibeon His 'strange work' and 'alien task' -- warfare is not God's natural work but an unusual act.

verses.text: As they fled from before Israel, while they were at the descent of Beth Horon, Yahweh hurled down great stones from the sky on them to Azekah, and they died. There were more who died from the hailstones than those whom the children of Israel killed with the sword.
verses.explainer: As the Amorites flee down the road from Beth-horon, the LORD hurls large hailstones on them from the sky. More die from hailstones than from Israelite swords. God fights with weather.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The hailstone bombardment reveals a pattern in Joshua: God fights using both supernatural means (hailstones, stopped sun) and natural means (ambush strategy, forced marches). The divine warrior uses every available weapon.

verses.text: Then Joshua spoke to Yahweh in the day when Yahweh delivered up the Amorites before the children of Israel. He said in the sight of Israel, “Sun, stand still on Gibeon! You, moon, stop in the valley of Aijalon!”
verses.explainer: On the day the LORD gives the Amorites over to Israel, Joshua says to the LORD in the presence of Israel: 'Sun, stand still over Gibeon, and you, moon, over the Valley of Aijalon.' The most famous prayer in Joshua.

thenVsNow.then: Joshua asked God to stop the sun and God did it. The audacity of the request and God's response demonstrate that prayer can change the course of nature itself. But notice: Joshua prayed in the context of faithful obedience, not presumption.
thenVsNow.now: Joshua asked God to stop the sun and God did it. The audacity of the request and God's response demonstrate that prayer can change the course of nature itself. But notice: Joshua prayed in the context of faithful obedience, not presumption.

verses.text: The sun stood still, and the moon stayed, until the nation had avenged themselves of their enemies. Isn’t this written in the book of Jashar? The sun stayed in the middle of the sky, and didn’t hurry to go down about a whole day.
verses.explainer: The sun stops and the moon stands still until the nation avenges itself. The sun stops in the middle of the sky and delays going down about a full day. A unique day in history.

crossReferences.text: The prophet references this event: 'Sun and moon stood still in the heavens at the glint of your flying arrows.'

verses.text: There was no day like that before it or after it, that Yahweh listened to the voice of a man; for Yahweh fought for Israel.
verses.explainer: There has never been a day like it before or since, a day when the LORD listened to a human being. Surely the LORD was fighting for Israel. God responds to a human command.

verses.text: Joshua returned, and all Israel with him, to the camp to Gilgal.
verses.explainer: Then Joshua returns with all Israel to the camp at Gilgal. The campaign concludes with a return to base.

verses.text: These five kings fled, and hid themselves in the cave at Makkedah.
verses.explainer: The five kings flee and hide in a cave at Makkedah. Their flight leaves their armies leaderless.

verses.text: Joshua was told, saying, “The five kings have been found, hidden in the cave at Makkedah.”
verses.explainer: Joshua is told that the five kings have been found hiding in a cave at Makkedah. The fugitive kings are discovered.

verses.text: Joshua said, “Roll large stones to cover the cave’s entrance, and set men by it to guard them;
verses.explainer: Joshua says: 'Roll large rocks up to the mouth of the cave, and post some men to guard it.' The kings are trapped while Israel finishes the pursuit.

verses.text: but don’t stay there. Pursue your enemies, and attack them from the rear. Don’t allow them to enter into their cities; for Yahweh your God has delivered them into your hand.”
verses.explainer: 'Don't stop; pursue your enemies and attack them from the rear. Don't let them reach their cities.' Tactical urgency: destroy the enemy army before it can regroup behind city walls.

verses.text: When Joshua and the children of Israel had finished killing them with a very great slaughter until they were consumed, and the remnant which remained of them had entered into the fortified cities,
verses.explainer: Joshua and the Israelites destroy the enemy army completely, though a few survivors manage to reach their fortified cities. The open-field victory is decisive.

verses.text: all the people returned to the camp to Joshua at Makkedah in peace. None moved his tongue against any of the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: The whole army returns safely to Joshua at Makkedah. No one utters a word against Israel. The victory is so complete that even criticism is silenced.

verses.text: Then Joshua said, “Open the cave entrance, and bring those five kings out of the cave to me.”
verses.explainer: Joshua orders: 'Open the mouth of the cave and bring those five kings out to me.' The moment of royal judgment arrives.

verses.text: They did so, and brought those five kings out of the cave to him: the king of Jerusalem, the king of Hebron, the king of Jarmuth, the king of Lachish, and the king of Eglon.
verses.explainer: The five kings are brought out: the kings of Jerusalem, Hebron, Jarmuth, Lachish, and Eglon. They face Joshua as captives.

verses.text:
When they brought those kings out to Joshua, Joshua called for all the men of Israel, and said to the chiefs of the men of war who went with him, “Come near. Put your feet on the necks of these kings.”
They came near, and put their feet on their necks.
verses.explainer: Joshua calls all Israel and tells his army commanders: 'Come and put your feet on the necks of these kings.' They do so. This is a symbolic act of total dominion.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Placing feet on the necks of defeated kings was a widespread ancient Near Eastern symbol of total victory. Egyptian and Assyrian art frequently depicts pharaohs and kings with their feet on prostrate enemies.

verses.text: Joshua said to them, “Don’t be afraid, nor be dismayed. Be strong and courageous, for Yahweh will do this to all your enemies against whom you fight.”
verses.explainer: Joshua says: 'Do not be afraid; do not be discouraged. Be strong and courageous. This is what the LORD will do to all the enemies you fight.' The defeated kings become an object lesson.

verses.text: Afterward Joshua struck them, put them to death, and hanged them on five trees. They were hanging on the trees until the evening.
verses.explainer: Joshua strikes and kills the five kings. He impales their bodies on five poles until evening. At sunset, the bodies are taken down and thrown into the cave, which is sealed with large rocks.

verses.text: At the time of the going down of the sun, Joshua commanded, and they took them down off the trees, and threw them into the cave in which they had hidden themselves, and laid great stones on the mouth of the cave, which remain to this very day.
verses.explainer: Joshua captures Makkedah and puts it to the sword, devoting the city to the LORD. The city that sheltered the fugitive kings falls first.

verses.text: Joshua took Makkedah on that day, and struck it with the edge of the sword, with its king. He utterly destroyed it and all the souls who were in it. He left no one remaining. He did to the king of Makkedah as he had done to the king of Jericho.
verses.explainer: The same day Joshua takes Makkedah, devoting it to total destruction. He treats its king as he treated the king of Jericho.

verses.text: Joshua passed from Makkedah, and all Israel with him, to Libnah, and fought against Libnah.
verses.explainer: Joshua moves from Makkedah to Libnah and attacks it. The LORD gives it and its king to Israel. Everyone is put to the sword.

verses.text: Yahweh delivered it also, with its king, into the hand of Israel. He struck it with the edge of the sword, and all the souls who were in it. He left no one remaining in it. He did to its king as he had done to the king of Jericho.
verses.explainer: Libnah falls just as Jericho did. The campaign systematically reduces the southern cities.

verses.text: Joshua passed from Libnah, and all Israel with him, to Lachish, and encamped against it, and fought against it.
verses.explainer: From Libnah, Joshua moves to Lachish and besieges it. Lachish was one of the most heavily fortified cities in Canaan.

verses.text: Yahweh delivered Lachish into the hand of Israel. He took it on the second day, and struck it with the edge of the sword, with all the souls who were in it, according to all that he had done to Libnah.
verses.explainer: The LORD gives Lachish to Israel on the second day. The quick capture of a major fortress demonstrates God's power.

verses.text: Then Horam king of Gezer came up to help Lachish; and Joshua struck him and his people, until he had left him no one remaining.
verses.explainer: Horam king of Gezer comes to help Lachish, but Joshua defeats him and his army, leaving no survivors. Reinforcements are destroyed before they can help.

verses.text: Joshua passed from Lachish, and all Israel with him, to Eglon; and they encamped against it and fought against it.
verses.explainer: From Lachish, Joshua moves to Eglon. They besiege it and take it that same day. The momentum is unstoppable.

verses.text: They took it on that day, and struck it with the edge of the sword. He utterly destroyed all the souls who were in it that day, according to all that he had done to Lachish.
verses.explainer: They capture Eglon, devoting it to total destruction, just as they did to Lachish. The campaign formula repeats with each city.

verses.text: Joshua went up from Eglon, and all Israel with him, to Hebron; and they fought against it.
verses.explainer: From Eglon, Joshua goes up to Hebron. The ancient city of Abraham is targeted.

verses.text: They took it, and struck it with the edge of the sword, with its king and all its cities, and all the souls who were in it. He left no one remaining, according to all that he had done to Eglon; but he utterly destroyed it, and all the souls who were in it.
verses.explainer: They take Hebron and its surrounding villages. Every person is put to the sword, just as at Eglon. No survivors remain.

verses.text: Joshua returned, and all Israel with him, to Debir, and fought against it.
verses.explainer: Joshua turns to Debir and attacks it. The southernmost major city is the final target.

verses.text: He took it, with its king and all its cities. They struck them with the edge of the sword, and utterly destroyed all the souls who were in it. He left no one remaining. As he had done to Hebron, so he did to Debir, and to its king; as he had done also to Libnah, and to its king.
verses.explainer: He takes Debir, its king, and all its villages, devoting them to destruction. No survivors. He does to Debir as he did to Hebron.

verses.text: So Joshua struck all the land, the hill country, the South, the lowland, the slopes, and all their kings. He left no one remaining, but he utterly destroyed all that breathed, as Yahweh, the God of Israel, commanded.
verses.explainer: So Joshua subdues the whole region: the hill country, the Negev, the western foothills, the mountain slopes, and all their kings. He leaves no survivors. The southern campaign summary.

verses.text: Joshua struck them from Kadesh Barnea even to Gaza, and all the country of Goshen, even to Gibeon.
verses.explainer: Joshua conquers from Kadesh-barnea to Gaza, and from the whole region of Goshen to Gibeon. The geographic scope is comprehensive.

verses.text: Joshua took all these kings and their land at one time because Yahweh, the God of Israel, fought for Israel.
verses.explainer: All these kings and their lands Joshua conquers in one campaign because the LORD, the God of Israel, fights for Israel. The theological explanation: divine warrior, not human armies.

verses.text: Joshua returned, and all Israel with him, to the camp to Gilgal.
verses.explainer: Then Joshua returns with all Israel to the camp at Gilgal. The southern campaign is complete; the army returns to base.

explainer.summary: Chapter 11 describes the northern campaign against the coalition led by Jabin of Hazor. Despite facing the largest enemy army yet -- with horses and chariots -- Joshua achieves a decisive victory through surprise attack. The chapter concludes with a comprehensive summary of the conquest, noting that the entire land was taken and the Anakite giants were destroyed.
themes: God's victory over overwhelming military power
Destroying military technology rather than adopting it
The completion of the conquest campaign
Faithfulness to Moses' commands throughout
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 11 completes the military conquest phase of the book. With the north subdued, the focus shifts to dividing the land. The chapter's summary statements (vv. 16-23) provide closure to the conquest narrative.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To cut the tendon of a horse's hind leg, permanent
wordStudies.meaning: To cut the tendon of a horse's hind leg, permanently disabling it. God commanded this rather than keeping the horses, ensuring Israel would rely on Him rather than on military technology. A costly act of trust.
wordStudies.significance: To cut the tendon of a horse's hind leg, permanently disabling it. God commanded this rather than ke

wordStudies.translatedAs: Cessation of military conflict
wordStudies.meaning: Cessation of military conflict. The land having 'rest from war' is a key formula in Joshua and Judges, describing the ideal state where God's people live securely in God's land under God's covenant.
wordStudies.significance: Cessation of military conflict. The land having 'rest from war' is a key formula in Joshua and Judge

verses.text: When Jabin king of Hazor heard of it, he sent to Jobab king of Madon, to the king of Shimron, to the king of Achshaph,
verses.explainer: When Jabin king of Hazor hears of Israel's victories, he sends word to the kings of the north: Madon, Shimron, Akshaph, and others. The last great coalition forms.

verses.text: and to the kings who were on the north, in the hill country, in the Arabah south of Chinneroth, in the lowland, and in the heights of Dor on the west,
verses.explainer: He rallies kings from the northern hill country, the Arabah south of Kinnereth, the western foothills, and the heights of Dor. The northern alliance is geographically comprehensive.

verses.text: to the Canaanite on the east and on the west, the Amorite, the Hittite, the Perizzite, the Jebusite in the hill country, and the Hivite under Hermon in the land of Mizpah.
verses.explainer: Canaanites from east and west, Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Jebusites in the hills, and Hivites below Hermon -- a vast multinational coalition assembles.

verses.text: They went out, they and all their armies with them, many people, even as the sand that is on the seashore in multitude, with very many horses and chariots.
verses.explainer: They come out with all their troops and a huge number of horses and chariots -- as numerous as sand on the seashore. The largest army Israel has yet faced.

verses.text: All these kings met together; and they came and encamped together at the waters of Merom, to fight with Israel.
verses.explainer: All these kings join forces and camp together at the Waters of Merom to fight Israel. The combined force is overwhelming by human calculation.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Joshua, “Don’t be afraid because of them; for tomorrow at this time, I will deliver them up all slain before Israel. You shall hamstring their horses and burn their chariots with fire.”
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Joshua: 'Do not be afraid of them. By this time tomorrow I will hand all of them, slain, over to Israel. You are to hamstring their horses and burn their chariots.' God's assurance precedes battle.

verses.text: So Joshua came suddenly, with all the warriors, against them by the waters of Merom, and attacked them.
verses.explainer: Joshua and his army march to the Waters of Merom and attack them suddenly. Another surprise attack, this time against the largest force yet.

verses.text: Yahweh delivered them into the hand of Israel, and they struck them, and chased them to great Sidon, and to Misrephoth Maim, and to the valley of Mizpah eastward. They struck them until they left them no one remaining.
verses.explainer: The LORD gives them into Israel's hand. Israel defeats them and pursues them to Great Sidon, Misrephoth-maim, and the Valley of Mizpah. No survivors remain.

verses.text: Joshua did to them as Yahweh told him. He hamstrung their horses and burned their chariots with fire.
verses.explainer: Joshua does to them as the LORD directed: he hamstrings the horses and burns the chariots. Israel deliberately destroys the military technology rather than adopting it.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Hamstringing horses and burning chariots was militarily irrational -- these were the most valuable military assets in the ancient world. God's command ensured Israel would fight as an infantry army dependent on divine help, not as a chariot-equipped conventional force.

thenVsNow.then: God commanded Israel to destroy horses and chariots rather than keep them. Trusting in technology rather than in God is a perennial temptation. The resources we accumulate for security can become substitutes for faith.
thenVsNow.now: God commanded Israel to destroy horses and chariots rather than keep them. Trusting in technology rather than in God is a perennial temptation. The resources we accumulate for security can become substitutes for faith.

verses.text: Joshua turned back at that time, and took Hazor, and struck its king with the sword: for Hazor used to be the head of all those kingdoms.
verses.explainer: Joshua turns back and captures Hazor. He puts its king to the sword. Hazor was the head of all these kingdoms -- the capital falls.

verses.text: They struck all the souls who were in it with the edge of the sword, utterly destroying them. There was no one left who breathed. He burned Hazor with fire.
verses.explainer: Everyone in Hazor is put to the sword. It is totally destroyed by fire. Hazor alone is burned -- the other cities on their mounds are left intact.

verses.text: Joshua captured all the cities of those kings, with their kings, and he struck them with the edge of the sword, and utterly destroyed them; as Moses the servant of Yahweh commanded.
verses.explainer: Joshua takes all the royal cities and their kings, putting them to the sword. He totally destroys them, as Moses commanded.

verses.text: But as for the cities that stood on their mounds, Israel burned none of them, except Hazor only. Joshua burned that.
verses.explainer: Israel does not burn any of the cities built on mounds -- except Hazor, which Joshua burns. The strategic cities are preserved for later use.

verses.text: The children of Israel took all the plunder of these cities, with the livestock, as plunder for themselves; but every man they struck with the edge of the sword, until they had destroyed them. They didn’t leave any who breathed.
verses.explainer: Israel carries off all the plunder and livestock for themselves but puts every person to the sword. The conquest allows taking goods but not sparing lives.

verses.text: As Yahweh commanded Moses his servant, so Moses commanded Joshua. Joshua did so. He left nothing undone of all that Yahweh commanded Moses.
verses.explainer: As the LORD commanded Moses, so Moses commanded Joshua. And Joshua does it, leaving nothing undone. The chain of obedience: God to Moses to Joshua.

verses.text: So Joshua captured all that land, the hill country, all the South, all the land of Goshen, the lowland, the Arabah, the hill country of Israel, and the lowland of the same;
verses.explainer: Joshua takes all this land: the hill country, the Negev, Goshen, the western foothills, the Arabah, and the mountains and foothills of Israel. A comprehensive geographic summary.

verses.text: from Mount Halak, that goes up to Seir, even to Baal Gad in the valley of Lebanon under Mount Hermon. He took all their kings, struck them, and put them to death.
verses.explainer: The territory extends from Mount Halak, which rises toward Seir, to Baal-gad in the Valley of Lebanon below Mount Hermon. The full extent of the conquest is described.

verses.text: Joshua made war a long time with all those kings.
verses.explainer: Joshua wages war against all these kings for a long time. The conquest was not instantaneous but a prolonged campaign.

verses.text: There was not a city that made peace with the children of Israel, except the Hivites, the inhabitants of Gibeon. They took all in battle.
verses.explainer: Except for the Hivites of Gibeon, no city makes peace with Israel. All others are taken in battle. Gibeon's treaty remains the sole exception.

verses.text: For it was of Yahweh to harden their hearts, to come against Israel in battle, that he might utterly destroy them, that they might have no favor, but that he might destroy them, as Yahweh commanded Moses.
verses.explainer: The LORD hardens the hearts of the Canaanites so they wage war against Israel, that they might be destroyed without mercy. God's sovereign will operates behind the military events.

verses.text: Joshua came at that time, and cut off the Anakim from the hill country, from Hebron, from Debir, from Anab, and from all the hill country of Judah, and from all the hill country of Israel: Joshua utterly destroyed them with their cities.
verses.explainer: Joshua also destroys the Anakites from the hill country: Hebron, Debir, Anab, and the hill country of Judah and Israel. The giants who terrified the earlier spies are eliminated.

crossReferences.text: The spies reported the Anakites as so large that 'we seemed like grasshoppers in our own eyes.' Joshua's generation proves the fear was unfounded.

verses.text: There were none of the Anakim left in the land of the children of Israel. Only in Gaza, in Gath, and in Ashdod, did some remain.
verses.explainer: No Anakites are left in Israelite territory. Some remain only in Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod -- Philistine cities. The giant tradition in Gath connects to Goliath centuries later.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The note that some Anakites survived in Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod connects to the later Goliath narrative. Goliath of Gath (1 Samuel 17) was likely descended from these surviving Anakites.

crossReferences.text: Goliath of Gath -- one of the cities where Anakites survived -- stood over nine feet tall, a descendant of the giants Joshua failed to eliminate completely.

verses.text: So Joshua took the whole land, according to all that Yahweh spoke to Moses; and Joshua gave it for an inheritance to Israel according to their divisions by their tribes. The land had rest from war.
verses.explainer: So Joshua takes the entire land, just as the LORD had directed Moses. He gives it as an inheritance to Israel according to their tribal divisions. Then the land has rest from war.

thenVsNow.then: The land has rest from war -- but the rest is not permanent because the conquest is not complete. Incomplete obedience produces temporary peace, not lasting security.
thenVsNow.now: The land has rest from war -- but the rest is not permanent because the conquest is not complete. Incomplete obedience produces temporary peace, not lasting security.

explainer.summary: Chapter 12 provides a comprehensive catalogue of the kings defeated by Moses (east of the Jordan) and Joshua (west of the Jordan). The list includes thirty-one kings west of the Jordan, representing a complete record of the conquest. This chapter serves as an inventory of God's faithfulness in giving Israel the promised land.
themes: The comprehensive record of divine victory
The cataloguing of fulfilled promises
East and west of the Jordan as complete conquest
Thirty-one kings defeated by divine power
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 12 serves as the hinge between the conquest narrative (chapters 1-11) and the land division narrative (chapters 13-21). It looks backward at what was accomplished before looking forward at what remains to be distributed.

wordStudies.translatedAs: An ancient people of great stature, also known as
wordStudies.meaning: An ancient people of great stature, also known as the 'shades' or 'ghosts' in some traditions. Og king of Bashan was one of the last Rephaites. His iron bed was reportedly 13 feet long (Deuteronomy 3:11).
wordStudies.significance: An ancient people of great stature, also known as the 'shades' or 'ghosts' in some traditions. Og ki

wordStudies.translatedAs: The total count represents the fragmented politica
wordStudies.meaning: The total count represents the fragmented political landscape of Canaan, where each city-state had its own king. Unlike Egypt or Mesopotamia with their empires, Canaan was a patchwork of small kingdoms -- making unified resistance difficult.

verses.text: Now these are the kings of the land, whom the children of Israel struck, and possessed their land beyond the Jordan toward the sunrise, from the valley of the Arnon to Mount Hermon, and all the Arabah eastward:
verses.explainer: These are the kings of the land whom the Israelites defeated and whose territory they took east of the Jordan. The catalogue begins with Moses' conquests in the Transjordan.

verses.text: Sihon king of the Amorites, who lived in Heshbon, and ruled from Aroer, which is on the edge of the valley of the Arnon, and the middle of the valley, and half Gilead, even to the river Jabbok, the border of the children of Ammon;
verses.explainer: Sihon king of the Amorites, who reigned in Heshbon. He ruled from Aroer on the rim of the Arnon Gorge to the Jabbok River. His territory was extensive.

crossReferences.text: The defeats of Sihon and Og by Moses are recorded in Numbers. Joshua's list begins with these earlier conquests to show continuity.

verses.text: and the Arabah to the sea of Chinneroth, eastward, and to the sea of the Arabah, even the Salt Sea, eastward, the way to Beth Jeshimoth; and on the south, under the slopes of Pisgah:
verses.explainer: Sihon's domain extended over the eastern Arabah from the Sea of Kinnereth to the Sea of the Arabah (the Dead Sea), and eastward to Beth-jeshimoth and the slopes of Pisgah.

verses.text: and the border of Og king of Bashan, of the remnant of the Rephaim, who lived at Ashtaroth and at Edrei,
verses.explainer: Og king of Bashan, one of the last of the Rephaites, who reigned in Ashtaroth and Edrei. The Rephaites were an ancient people of great stature.

crossReferences.text: Og's iron bed was 13 feet long and 6 feet wide, indicating his extraordinary size.

verses.text: and ruled in Mount Hermon, and in Salecah, and in all Bashan, to the border of the Geshurites and the Maacathites, and half Gilead, the border of Sihon king of Heshbon.
verses.explainer: Og ruled over Mount Hermon, Salekah, all of Bashan to the border of the Geshurites and Maakathites, and half of Gilead to the border of Sihon. His territory was vast.

verses.text: Moses the servant of Yahweh and the children of Israel struck them. Moses the servant of Yahweh gave it for a possession to the Reubenites, and the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh.
verses.explainer: Moses, the servant of the LORD, and the Israelites conquered these two kings. He gave their territory to the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh. The Transjordan division is credited to Moses.

verses.text: These are the kings of the land whom Joshua and the children of Israel struck beyond the Jordan westward, from Baal Gad in the valley of Lebanon even to Mount Halak, that goes up to Seir. Joshua gave it to the tribes of Israel for a possession according to their divisions;
verses.explainer: Now begins the list of kings west of the Jordan whom Joshua and the Israelites conquered, from Baal-gad in the Valley of Lebanon to Mount Halak near Seir.

verses.text: in the hill country, and in the lowland, and in the Arabah, and in the slopes, and in the wilderness, and in the South; the Hittite, the Amorite, and the Canaanite, the Perizzite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite:
verses.explainer: This territory included the hill country, western foothills, Arabah, mountain slopes, wilderness, and Negev -- the land of the Hittites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites.

verses.text:
the king of Jericho, one;
the king of Ai, which is beside Bethel, one;
verses.explainer: The king of Jericho -- one. The king of Ai (near Bethel) -- one. The catalogue counts each defeated king individually.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The king list format is paralleled in Egyptian and Mesopotamian conquest records. Ancient kings commemorated their victories with similar catalogues, establishing their authority and legitimacy. Joshua's list credits God, not Joshua, as the victor.

verses.text:
the king of Jerusalem, one;
the king of Hebron, one;
verses.explainer: The king of Jerusalem -- one. The king of Hebron -- one. Major cities of the southern campaign are listed.

verses.text:
the king of Jarmuth, one;
the king of Lachish, one;
verses.explainer: The king of Jarmuth -- one. The king of Lachish -- one. The five kings of the Amorite coalition (chapter 10) appear in the list.

verses.text:
the king of Eglon, one;
the king of Gezer, one;
verses.explainer: The king of Eglon -- one. The king of Gezer -- one. The southern campaign cities continue.

verses.text:
the king of Debir, one;
the king of Geder, one;
verses.explainer: The king of Debir -- one. The king of Geder -- one. Further southern conquests are catalogued.

verses.text:
the king of Hormah, one;
the king of Arad, one;
verses.explainer: The king of Hormah -- one. The king of Arad -- one. Cities in the Negev region.

verses.text:
the king of Libnah, one;
the king of Adullam, one;
verses.explainer: The king of Libnah -- one. The king of Adullam -- one. Cities in the Shephelah (western foothills).

verses.text:
the king of Makkedah, one;
the king of Bethel, one;
verses.explainer: The king of Makkedah -- one. The king of Bethel -- one. The list connects to specific battle narratives in chapters 8-10.

verses.text:
the king of Tappuah, one;
the king of Hepher, one;
verses.explainer: The king of Tappuah -- one. The king of Hepher -- one. Central hill country cities.

verses.text:
the king of Aphek, one;
the king of Lassharon, one;
verses.explainer: The king of Aphek -- one. The king of Lasharon -- one. Coastal and northern cities.

verses.text:
the king of Madon, one;
the king of Hazor, one;
verses.explainer: The king of Madon -- one. The king of Hazor -- one. The northern campaign cities from chapter 11.

verses.text:
the king of Shimron Meron, one;
the king of Achshaph, one;
verses.explainer: The king of Shimron-meron -- one. The king of Akshaph -- one. Northern coalition members.

verses.text:
the king of Taanach, one;
the king of Megiddo, one;
verses.explainer: The king of Taanach -- one. The king of Megiddo -- one. Strategic cities controlling the Jezreel Valley.

verses.text:
the king of Kedesh, one;
the king of Jokneam in Carmel, one;
verses.explainer: The king of Kedesh -- one. The king of Jokneam in Carmel -- one. Northern Galilee and Carmel coast cities.

verses.text:
the king of Dor in the height of Dor, one;
the king of Goiim in Gilgal, one;
verses.explainer: The king of Dor (in Naphoth Dor) -- one. The king of Goyim in Gilgal -- one. Coastal cities.

verses.text:
the king of Tirzah, one:
all the kings thirty-one.
verses.explainer: The king of Tirzah -- one. Thirty-one kings in all. The grand total: thirty-one kings defeated, thirty-one kingdoms conquered. The conquest is summarized in a single number.

thenVsNow.then: Thirty-one kings in a relatively small territory reveals the extreme political fragmentation of Canaan. Each city-state acted independently, making unified resistance nearly impossible. God's timing placed the conquest in a period of maximum Canaanite vulnerability.
thenVsNow.now: Thirty-one kings in a relatively small territory reveals the extreme political fragmentation of Canaan. Each city-state acted independently, making unified resistance nearly impossible. God's timing placed the conquest in a period of maximum Canaanite vulnerability.

explainer.summary: Chapter 13 begins the land distribution section by noting the land that remains unconquered and describing the inheritance of the Transjordan tribes (Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh). God reminds Joshua that much land still needs to be taken, even though the major campaigns are over.
themes: Land remaining to be conquered
The Transjordan inheritance
God's promise versus current reality
The Levites' unique inheritance: God Himself
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 13 transitions from conquest to distribution. It honestly acknowledges that the conquest is incomplete while proceeding with the division. This tension between promise and fulfillment runs through the rest of the book.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A permanent, hereditary land grant
wordStudies.meaning: A permanent, hereditary land grant. Each tribe's inheritance was not just real estate but a divine gift that embodied God's promise to Abraham. It could not be permanently sold because it belonged ultimately to God.
wordStudies.significance: A permanent, hereditary land grant. Each tribe's inheritance was not just real estate but a divine g

wordStudies.translatedAs: The tribe of Levi received no territorial inherita
wordStudies.meaning: The tribe of Levi received no territorial inheritance. Instead, they received designated cities and pasturelands. Their 'inheritance' was the LORD Himself and the offerings made to Him -- a spiritual rather than territorial provision.
wordStudies.significance: The tribe of Levi received no territorial inheritance. Instead, they received designated cities and

verses.text: Now Joshua was old and well advanced in years. Yahweh said to him, “You are old and advanced in years, and there remains yet very much land to be possessed.
verses.explainer: When Joshua is old and advanced in years, the LORD tells him: 'You are now very old, and there are still very large areas of land to be taken over.' The honest assessment of unfinished business.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Joshua's advanced age (probably 90+) and the remaining unconquered territory create urgency. The land must be distributed even though the conquest is incomplete, because Joshua may not live to finish it.

crossReferences.text: The book of Judges catalogs the specific territories each tribe failed to conquer -- the consequences of the incomplete conquest described here.

thenVsNow.then: The admission that much remains undone despite great accomplishments is honest leadership. Joshua does not pretend the job is finished; he distributes what is available while acknowledging what remains.
thenVsNow.now: The admission that much remains undone despite great accomplishments is honest leadership. Joshua does not pretend the job is finished; he distributes what is available while acknowledging what remains.

verses.text: “This is the land that still remains: all the regions of the Philistines, and all the Geshurites;
verses.explainer: This verse continues the description of tribal boundaries and cities for the land allotment in chapter 13. These geographic details established legal property rights for each tribe, ensuring that every family had a specific, identifiable inheritance in the promised land.

verses.explainer: This verse continues the description of tribal boundaries and cities for the land allotment in chapter 13. These geographic details established legal property rights for each tribe, ensuring that every family had a specific, identifiable inheritance in the promised land.

verses.explainer: This verse continues the description of tribal boundaries and cities for the land allotment in chapter 13. These geographic details established legal property rights for each tribe, ensuring that every family had a specific, identifiable inheritance in the promised land.

verses.explainer: This verse continues the description of tribal boundaries and cities for the land allotment in chapter 13. These geographic details established legal property rights for each tribe, ensuring that every family had a specific, identifiable inheritance in the promised land.

verses.text: all the kingdom of Og in Bashan, who reigned in Ashtaroth and in Edrei (the same was left of the remnant of the Rephaim); for Moses attacked these, and drove them out.

verses.text: Nevertheless the children of Israel didn’t drive out the Geshurites, nor the Maacathites: but Geshur and Maacath live within Israel to this day.
verses.explainer: This verse continues the description of tribal boundaries and cities for the land allotment in chapter 13. These geographic details established legal property rights for each tribe, ensuring that every family had a specific, identifiable inheritance in the promised land.

verses.text: Only he gave no inheritance to the tribe of Levi. The offerings of Yahweh, the God of Israel, made by fire are his inheritance, as he spoke to him.
verses.explainer: But to the tribe of Levi, Joshua gives no inheritance. The food offerings presented to the LORD, the God of Israel, are their inheritance, as God promised. The Levites' provision is God Himself.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Priestly tribes in the ancient Near East often held significant land and wealth. Israel's system -- dispersing the Levites and making them dependent on tithes and offerings -- prevented the development of an independent priestly estate.

crossReferences.text: God told Aaron: 'You will have no inheritance in their land. I am your share and your inheritance among the Israelites.'

thenVsNow.then: The Levites' inheritance was God Himself -- not land, not material security, but relationship with the divine. This challenges any equation of blessing with material prosperity.
thenVsNow.now: The Levites' inheritance was God Himself -- not land, not material security, but relationship with the divine. This challenges any equation of blessing with material prosperity.

explainer.summary: Chapter 14 introduces the western land distribution at Gilgal and features Caleb's claim to Hebron. At age 85, Caleb demands the mountain territory where the Anakite giants live, declaring that he is as strong as he was forty-five years earlier when he spied out the land.
